title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
text: 12 April 2026 TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks Kangan Li 1, Tushar Mittal 1, Benjamin K. Holtzman 2, Seth Saltiel 3, Raphael Aп¬ғnito 1,4 1. Department of Geosciences, Pennsylvania State University 2. Lamont Doherty Earth Observatory, Columbia University 3. Department of Earth and Atmospheric Sciences, Cornell University 4. GFZ Helmholtz Center for Geosciences Abstract Modeling the mechanical deformation of geological materials across the vast range of relevant thermodynamic conditions and dynamic processes remains a fundamental challenge in Earth sciences. Conventional rheological models often rely on isolated disciplinary approaches and hard-coded iterative solvers that struggle with strongly coupled, non-linear constitutive laws. Furthermore, inverting macroscopic laboratory data to infer sub-grid micro-physics is fundamentally ill-posed, leading to structural non-uniqueness and models that may violate thermodynamic laws during transient loading. To address these limitations, we present TIP-INN (Thermodynamics of Irreversible Processes Informed Neural Network), a thermodynamically consistent machine learning framework grounded in the Generalized Standard Materials (GSM) formalism. TIP-INN discovers constitutive laws directly from time-series stress-strain data by learning two strictly convex scalar thermodynamic potentials-the Helmholtz free energy and the dual dissipation potential-architecturally guaranteeing non-negative mechanical dissipation. By utilizing a continuous, diп¬Җerentiable neural integrator to solve internal state variable (ISV) evolution as an initial-value problem, TIP-INN enables stable rapid training across complex rheologies. We resolve the inherent non-uniqueness of the constitutive inverse problem through two regularizing strategies: (1) utilizing diverse loading protocols (e.g., cyclic and stressrelaxation paths) to map the full topological state space of the potentials, and (2) a multimodal loss formulation that directly assimilates auxiliary seismo-acoustic data. Data may be preliminary. вҖ” https://doi.org/10.22541/essoar.15001844/v1 PNAS Nexus, 2026, pp. 1вҖ“25 doi: Paper TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks Kangan Li,a Tushar Mittal*,a Benjamin K. Holtzman,b Seth Saltielc and Raphael Affinitoa,d aDepartment of Geosciences, The Pennsylvania State University, University Park, Pennsylvania 16802, bLamont Doherty Earth Observatory, Columbia University, Palisades, New York 10964, cDepartment of Earth and Atmospheric Sciences, Cornell University, Ithaca, New York 14850 and dGFZ Helmholtz Center for Geosciences, Potsdam, Germany Tushar Mittal: tmittal@psu.edu Abstract Modeling the mechanical deformation of geological materials across the vast range of relevant thermodynamic conditions and dynamic processes remains a fundamental challenge in Earth sciences. Conventional rheological models often rely on isolated disciplinary approaches and hard-coded iterative solvers that struggle with strongly coupled, non-linear constitutive laws. Furthermore, inverting macroscopic laboratory data to infer sub-grid micro-physics is fundamentally ill-posed, leading to structural non-uniqueness and models that may violate thermodynamic laws during transient loading. To address these limitations, we present TIP-INN (Thermodynamics of Irreversible Processes Informed Neural Network), a thermodynamically consistent machine learning framework grounded in the Generalized Standard Materials (GSM) formalism. TIP-INN discovers constitutive laws directly from time-series stress-strain data by learning two strictly convex scalar thermodynamic potentials-the Helmholtz free energy and the dual dissipation potential-architecturally guaranteeing non-negative mechanical dissipation. By utilizing a continuous, differentiable neural integrator to solve internal state variable (ISV) evolution as an initial-value problem, TIP-INN enables stable rapid training across complex rheologies. We resolve the inherent non-uniqueness of the constitutive inverse problem through two regularizing strategies: (1) utilizing diverse loading protocols (e.g., cyclic and stress-relaxation paths) to map the full topological state space of the potentials, and (2) a multi-modal loss formulation that directly assimilates auxiliary seismo-acoustic data. Thus, TIP-INN provides a mathematically rigorous bridge between continuum damage mechanics and laboratory seismology. Finally, to ensure physical interpretability, the learned potentials are distilled via a Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Potentials (SINP) procedure into closed-form analytical expressions enabling efficient implementation in geodynamic codes. title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
text: (SINP) procedure into closed-form analytical expressions enabling efficient implementation in geodynamic codes. Key words: constitutive model discovery, thermodynamics-informed neural networks, convex neural networks, general viscoelasticity, continuum damage mechanics Significance statement Predictive modeling of Earth systems-from earthquakes to geothermal reservoirs-is limited by empirical rock mechanics laws that fail to capture complex, transient deformation. We introduce a machine learning framework (TIP-INN) that embeds thermodynamic laws directly into its architecture, guaranteeing mechanical consistency by construction rather than by soft penalty. A novel aspect of this framework is that it can bridge continuum mechanics and observational seismology by assimilating data from the seismo-acoustic wavefield reflecting the evolution of internal rock structure to constrain unobservable internal rock damage. By combining thermodynamically diverse loading protocols with seismo-acoustic assimilation, TIP-INN provides a computationally efficient pathway for discovering unified constitutive laws governing the dynamic Earth, with direct applicability to tectonic deformation, geothermal energy, and earthquake physics. Introduction The evolution of Earth systems-ranging from the flow of ice streams to mantle convection-fundamentally depends on how geological materials respond to changes in stress (including pressure), temperature, and chemistry. Consequently, the accuracy of large-scale geophysical models strongly relies on constitutive laws that can accurately map deformation to the stress tensor, Пғ, across diverse planetary conditions. In crustal tectonics, the formation of shear zones by strain localization is driven by feedbacks between microstructural evolution and mechanical strength, spanning brittle to ductile processes[10, 9]. Volcanic eruptive dynamics are governed by the non-linear rheology of magmas transitioning from fluid suspensions to granular solids with increasing crystallinity [12]. Similarly, at the EarthвҖҷs surface, subglacial slip behaviors are modeled using a combination of viscous and plastic laws, directly impacting global sea level rise predictions [85]. Despite their importance, deriving these constitutive laws is difficult because geophysics currently lacks a unified framework for material behavior. Instead, disciplines rely on isolated approaches for similar processes: glaciologists and geodynamicists generally use a different empirical flow laws [e.g. 4] for the same ensemble of processes. To describe localized, discrete stick-slip failure, seismologists use rate-and-state friction [27, 81, 78, 54], while geodynamicists typically default to plasticity (a time-averaged, spatial-homogenized description of many discrete failures inside a representative volume) [e.g. 9, 38], because of their different scientific aims. Beyond fundamental Earth science, bridging this disciplinary gap is also necessary for applied subsurface engineering applications related to energy extraction and storage. Focus areas such as Superhot Enhanced Geothermal Systems and geologic carbon storage

title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
text: many discrete failures inside a representative volume) [e.g. 9, 38], because of their different scientific aims. Beyond fundamental Earth science, bridging this disciplinary gap is also necessary for applied subsurface engineering applications related to energy extraction and storage. Focus areas such as Superhot Enhanced Geothermal Systems and geologic carbon storage require predictive models that operate well outside the steady-state conditions typical of many laboratory datasets [92, 69]. Generalized constitutive models for geologic solids must be capable of spanning diverse thermodynamic regimes and the entire mechanical spectrum of deformation processes and microstructural evolution. Geological materials can exhibit profound mechanical shift across relatively small ranges of conditions, for example, transitioning from localized brittle cracking in the shallow crust to distributed ductile creep at greater depths, or even in response to different forcing rates on the same rock volume. Capturing these complex brittle-ductile transitions requires bridging a large set of length scales and integrating a range of processes. Instead of attempting to model every individual micro-crack or dislocation glide [42], one common theoretical approach is to represent these sub-grid processes using internal state variables (ISVs) averaged over a Representative Elementary Volume (REV). These variables can track some aspect of the microstructure, but also partitioning among different mechanisms. By tracking variables such as scalar damage (Оұ) for brittle degradation and the visco-plastic strain tensor (Оөp) for ductile flow, distinct micro-mechanisms can be integrated into a single continuum model. The Generalized Standard Materials (GSM) framework [39, 37] is one such formalism for deforming nonlinear solids, utilized here. At the macro-scale (km), solving the conservation laws (e.g., mass-momentum-energy conservation) requires constitutive closures that couple the evolution of these ISVs to macroscopic stress and strain. Unlike homogeneous engineered materials, crustal rocks are intrinsically multimineralic and multi-scale, making the derivation of these constitutive closures non-trivial and often non-unique. Deriving these closures presents two main physical challenges. First, conventional approaches often rely on вҖңpatchwork" multiphysics models that couple disparate empirical laws and use ad hoc or simplified criteria to switch between elastic, plastic, and viscous regimes. Because these models lack a unified energy budget, they can inadvertently violate the second law of thermodynamics especially during transient loading, potentially predicting physically impossible states like negative mechanical dissipation [20, 65]. Second, a fundamental scale gap exists between macroscopic observations and sub-grid defect dynamics. Because standard laboratory measurements of macroscopic stress and strain cannot uniquely constrain internal micro-physics, researchers typically pre-select rigid

title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
text: second law of thermodynamics especially during transient loading, potentially predicting physically impossible states like negative mechanical dissipation [20, 65]. Second, a fundamental scale gap exists between macroscopic observations and sub-grid defect dynamics. Because standard laboratory measurements of macroscopic stress and strain cannot uniquely constrain internal micro-physics, researchers typically pre-select rigid phenomenological models-such as specific combinations of viscoelastic spring-and-dashpot elements (e.g., Maxwell, Kelvin-Voight, or Burgers) to fit data. This rigidity introduces a strong structural bias. While one can perfectly match an experimental curve by arbitrarily adding mechanical elements, this over-parameterization makes the resulting rheology highly dependent on the initial model choice. It obscures the true, often sparser physics driving the deformation and yields empirical fits that fail to extrapolate reliably beyond the laboratory [52]. Addressing this challenge requires a data-driven constitutive model discovery method that is mathematically flexible yet strictly obeys thermodynamic laws, capable of capturing complex damage mechanics without presupposing a specific physical structure. Machine learning offers a potential solution to these bottlenecks, but many data-driven methods struggle with physical consistency. Purely empirical models and standard physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) that rely on soft loss penalties [74] are prone to overfitting. When applied to geological media, they often predict physically impossible states-such as spontaneous crack healing or negative dissipation (see Supplement for rate-state friction case), which compromises their ability to extrapolate beyond their training data [53, 36]. Although thermodynamically consistent neural architectures have successfully modeled engineered materials like metals and elastomers [64, 32, 58, 33, 45, 80, 48], they have not yet been adapted for the complex, multi-mineralic nature of rocks. To address these methodological gaps, this study introduces the Thermodynamics of Irreversible Processes Informed Neural Network (TIP-INN). Built on the differentiable programming (вҲӮP) paradigm [86], TIP-INN discovers constitutive laws by learning two strictly convex scalar thermodynamic potentials: the Helmholtz free energy, and the dual dissipation potential, By parameterizing these potentials with strictly convex neural networks [3], guarantees thermodynamic consistency by construction. Automatic differentiation of these potentials yields the thermodynamic driving forces, Xd = вҲ’вҲӮОЁ/вҲӮОұ, governing the evolution of the internal state variables while ensuring positive intrinsic dissipation (D вүҘ0) across all predictions. This architectural enforcement of thermodynamic constraints-as opposed to soft loss-penalty approaches-is critical for stability and extrapolation reliability beyond laboratory training datasets. Furthermore, classical constitutive updates for complex materials rely on iterative numerical solvers (like NewtonRaphson) that require the derivation of algorithmic tangents, are hard-coded to specific formulations,

text: across all predictions. This architectural enforcement of thermodynamic constraints-as opposed to soft loss-penalty approaches-is critical for stability and extrapolation reliability beyond laboratory training datasets. Furthermore, classical constitutive updates for complex materials rely on iterative numerical solvers (like NewtonRaphson) that require the derivation of algorithmic tangents, are hard-coded to specific formulations, and frequently fail to converge during complex transient loading. To overcome this, TIP-INN treats the constitutive update as an initialvalue Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) solved via a В© The Authors. All rights reserved. Fig. 1. Bottom Right: The detailed architecture of a convex neural network used for learning the constitutive model. fully differentiable neural integrator [15, 63]. This endto-end continuous gradient propagation circumvents the computational overhead and convergence failures of hard-coded solvers, enabling faster, more stable training over complex deformation histories as long as we do not consider structural state transitions and stiffness of the mechanical evolution equations. Because the integrator is fully differentiable, the network intrinsically learns path-dependence, hysteresis, and fading memory-features that are critical for geological deformation [43] but mathematically invisible to standard feed-forward architectures for learning stress-strain data pairs. Crucially, to resolve the macroscopic scale gap, we can integrate multi-modal seismo-acoustic data-such as acoustic emissions (AE), seismic and ultrasonic wave velocities (Vp, Vs), and waveform features-directly into the TIP-INN modelвҖҷs objective function [? ? ]. While macroscopic stress alone leaves the evolution of internal variables unconstrained, seismo-acoustic data provides a direct, observable proxy for sub-grid defect dynamics and the internal damage state. Motivated by rapid advances in observational geophysicsincluding Distributed Acoustic Sensing (DAS) and microearthquake monitoring-this multi-modal assimilation bridges the gap between lab-scale mechanics and field-scale observables. Combined with high-variance thermodynamic training paths (e.g., cyclic loading, stress-relaxation), this strategy effectively regularizes the ill-posed inversion of the unobservable ISVs. Finally, despite their flexibility, high-dimensional neural

title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
text: rapid advances in observational geophysicsincluding Distributed Acoustic Sensing (DAS) and microearthquake monitoring-this multi-modal assimilation bridges the gap between lab-scale mechanics and field-scale observables. Combined with high-variance thermodynamic training paths (e.g., cyclic loading, stress-relaxation), this strategy effectively regularizes the ill-posed inversion of the unobservable ISVs. Finally, despite their flexibility, high-dimensional neural networks are inherently difficult to interpret and often function as opaque "black boxes." To recover the underlying physics, we introduce a Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Potentials (SINP) approach, adapted from the SINDy framework [7]. SINP projects the trained neural potentials onto a constrained, strictly convex library of analytical functions, distilling the network into closed-form equations. Because this projection preserves thermodynamic admissibility, it yields explicit mathematical expressions for the non-linear coupling between macroscopic deformation and internal variable evolution. By recovering the exact functional forms of the free energy and dissipation potentials, we can directly analyze the specific damage mechanics and rheological transitions the network learned from the data. Moreover, these distilled analytical laws can be used in existing computational geodynamics codes [2]. In this study, we apply the TIP-INN framework to synthetic models (with known underlying potentials) and complex, multimodal laboratory datasets to address four specific scientific questions regarding constitutive discovery in rock mechanics: 1.Does the end-to-end differentiable Neural ODE framework provide a scalable, fast, and stable approach for modeling geologically relevant behaviors? 2.Given the structural non-uniqueness of the constitutive model state space, what diversity of experimental loading paths (monotonic, cyclic, stress-relaxation) is required to capture the true topology of the thermodynamic potentials? 3.Can the integration of ancillary seismo-acoustic data effectively regularize the ill-posed inversion of unobservable internal state variables in real, heterogeneous rocks? 4.Can high-dimensional neural potentials be distilled into closed-form, mathematically interpretable analytical equations using a thermodynamically constrained sparse regression The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. Section 3 evaluates the accuracy and robustness of the proposed computational framework by validating it against analytical models and demonstrating its capability to discover rheological models from experimental data. Section discusses the implications of this framework for upscaling to crustal-scale geophysical models. We conclude our work in Section 5. The theoretical background and detailed neural architecture are provided in Section 6. text: theoretical background and detailed neural architecture are provided in Section 6. TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks Overview of TIP-INN The Generalized Standard Material formalism constitutes a robust framework for constitutive modeling of solids, combining elasticity with a broad range of linear and non-linear dissipative mechanisms. The GSM is built upon fundamental thermodynamic energy potentials (e.g., the Helmholtz free energy, ОЁ) and the Dissipation potentials ОҰ (formally pseudo-potentials), which encode energy dissipation as functions of the fluxes of state variables, in a complementary construction to the energy potentials. Importantly, the gradients of these potentials define the conjugate thermodynamic forces governing the materialвҖҷs microstructural evolution. The free energy determines the reversible internal stresses (the energetic driving forces, AОұ), while the dissipation potential dictates the corresponding dissipative stresses (the resisting viscous or plastic drag forces, Xd). The strict balance between these internal stresses enforces local thermodynamic equilibrium. The detailed derivations and the physical interpretation of these terms within the context of specific rheological models are provided in Section 6 and Supplementary Material. One key advantage of working directly with thermodynamic potentials вҖ“rather than empirical stress-strain relationshipsвҖ“ is that the material response of generalized solids is governed by time-evolution equations derived strictly from the derivatives of these potentials (see Section 6). Similarly, macroscopic material properties (e.g., elastic modulus, heat capacity, viscosity, etc.) can be self-consistently derived based on the Hessian of the potentials (second derivatives with respect to the state variables for ОЁ and their forces or fluxes for ОҰ, explained further in Section 6). Thus, even if the model is trained exclusively on macroscopic stress-strain pairs, we can still mathematically represent the coupled underlying processes by learning a complete set of potentials, allowing us to capture the non-equilibrium evolution of internal state variables (ISVs) as paths through the two potentials. To operationally use this theory, we introduce a thermodynamically consistent neural network framework, the Thermodynamics of Irreversible Processes Informed Neural Network (TIP-INN), which pairs a neural ODE integrator [63, 33] with two parallel, strictly convex neural networks (see Figure 1). These networks are tasked with predicting the topologies of the Helmholtz free energy (ОЁ) and dual dissipation potential (ОҰвҲ—) under isothermal, infinitesimal strain conditions. Consequently, the training process becomes significantly faster and more robust across different rheologies using the exact same architecture. Provided the numerical time steps are sufficiently small, both inference and internal state evolution

title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
text: the topologies of the Helmholtz free energy (ОЁ) and dual dissipation potential (ОҰвҲ—) under isothermal, infinitesimal strain conditions. Consequently, the training process becomes significantly faster and more robust across different rheologies using the exact same architecture. Provided the numerical time steps are sufficiently small, both inference and internal state evolution reduce to a single forward pass through the network, entirely circumventing iterative convergence failures. We can use this directly into an solver (like torchdiffeq), rollout the trajectory, compute a loss against the true data, and backpropagate using the adjoint method to adapt the NN weights. Ultimately, the TIP-INN architecture enables the discovery of complex rheological models directly from sparse experimental datasets, such as stress-strain time series and seismo-acoustic data. By effectively assimilating diverse experimental loading paths (monotonic and cyclic), the framework captures underlying topologies of the thermodynamic potentials, resulting in accurate, thermodynamics-obeying constitutive models. Results Accuracy and Training Data Diversity: The Maxwell Model Before applying to complex experimental rock data, we first demonstrate its fundamental capability to discover hidden, but known, thermodynamic structures. We emphasize a core achievement of this framework: even when trained exclusively on macroscopic stress-strain data pairs, successfully reconstructs the true, unobservable topologies of both the Helmholtz free energy (ОЁ) and the dual dissipation potential (ОҰвҲ—). To rigorously validate this accuracy and robustness, we utilize potentials derived from standard analytical constitutive models вҖ“specifically, the Maxwell and Burgers viscoelastic models (see rheological representations in Supplementary Figure S1)вҖ“ which provide a known mathematical ground truth for these potentials. As a necessary first step, we utilize a standard Maxwell viscoelastic model (YoungвҖҷs modulus Ev = 2 GPa and viscosity О·v = 1 GPaВ·s) to systematically investigate the diversity of loading conditions strictly required for effective thermodynamic training. We consider two distinct strain-controlled loading modes: cyclic loads and monotonic loads (both with and without oscillations). First, evaluating monotonic strain path, Figure demonstrates that training TIP-INN on an oscillatory strains and testing it on a non-oscillatory strains produces more accurate ОЁ predictions than the reverse. We see similar results when comparing simple step-wise monotonic paths compared to more stochastic paths that sample a larger range of forcing frequencies and history dependence. This asymmetry occurs because the oscillatory load forces the network to explore a broader region of the materialвҖҷs thermodynamic state space. Second, training with a single cyclic load yields an accurate ОЁ for other cyclic tests of differing frequencies and magnitudes,

title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
text: a larger range of forcing frequencies and history dependence. This asymmetry occurs because the oscillatory load forces the network to explore a broader region of the materialвҖҷs thermodynamic state space. Second, training with a single cyclic load yields an accurate ОЁ for other cyclic tests of differing frequencies and magnitudes, but its precision degrades significantly when tested against a monotonically oscillatory load with large viscous strains. Third, training exclusively with a monotonically oscillatory load yields an accurate ОЁ for cyclic tests, but fails to extrapolate accurately to other monotonic tests with higher viscous strains. These results show how little data is needed to train a TIP-INN, because it is so highly constrained. Also, they highlight the benefits of training on diverse, multi-modal loading types to improve model transferability. As shown in Figure 2, training TIP-INN with a combined ensemble of 5 cyclic and 5 monotonically oscillatory loads consistently yields highly accurate stress and free energy predictions across all testing data, outperforming single-load training. The convex and positive properties of ОЁ and ОҰвҲ—remain wellpreserved. Most importantly, because TIP-INN learns the true topologies of the thermodynamic potentials rather than merely memorizing empirical stress-strain curves, it correctly predicts the underlying viscosity and YoungвҖҷs modulus. The minor oscillations in the predicted YoungвҖҷs modulus are numerical artifacts attributed to errors in computing the second-order derivatives of the free energy. These results carry direct, critical implications for experimental rock mechanics: discovering robust constitutive laws requires diverse experimental loading protocols to adequately sample the internal thermodynamic state space. Additional predicted results for the training data are provided in Supplementary Figure S3. Structural Non-Uniqueness and the Ill-Posed Inverse Problem: The Burgers Model Having established that diverse loading paths are required to capture the true topology of thermodynamic potentials, we now investigate how structural non-uniqueness affects constitutive discovery. We test the accuracy of TIP-INN on the more complex Burgers viscoelastic model with prescribed material properties (Ev1 = 1.0 GPa, О·v1 = 2.0 GPaВ·s, Ev2 = 2.0 GPa, and О·v2 = 0.5 GPaВ·s). The theoretical Burgers model contains two internal state variables (ISVs) associated with the viscosities О·v1 and О·v2. However, because TIP-INN does not know the exact rheological representation a priori, we test the network by assuming either one or two internal variables in the system, utilizing the robust, diverse training protocols established in the previous section. Figure 3 shows the predicted stress, Helmholtz free energy, and dual dissipation from TIP-INN across the testing data. The predicted macroscopic stresses are nearly indistinguishable from the true theoretical solutions, regardless of whether one or two ISVs are assumed. However, accurate Helmholtz free energy topologies are obtained only when a single ISV is assumed; large errors arise when two ISVs are introduced, particularly for monotonically oscillatory loads with high viscous strains. Consequently, the network predicts less accurate thermodynamic potentials (ОЁ and ОҰвҲ—), and large deviations emerge between the predicted and true material properties. This perfectly illustrates the fundamentally illposed nature of classical rheological inversion highlighted in the introduction: identical macroscopic stress-strain responses can be achieved through entirely different combinations of internal linear or nonlinear material properties. Additional predicted results for the training data are provided in Supplementary Figure S5. Discovering rheological models from experimental data We next apply TIP-INN to discover rheological models directly from experimental data. The dataset is derived from a uniaxial compression test performed on a porous cylindrical rock sample (p5914, 1 inch core and 2.5 inch long) cored from lava flows exposed along the southeast avalanche scarp of Pacaya volcano [22]. The experiment was conducted at the Penn State Rock Mechanics Deformation Apparatus under unconfined uniaxial loading at room temperature. Axial deformation was applied under displacement control along a monotonically increasing loading path. Servo-controlled hydraulic feedback was achieved using direct current displacement transducers (DCDTs) with a resolution of Вұ0.1, Вөm and a custom-built beryllium-copper load cell with a resolution of Вұ5 N. Throughout deformation, the sample response was monitored both mechanically and acoustically. To enable ultrasonic monitoring, the steel loading platens were fitted with in-house-built broadband P-wave leadzirconate-titanate (PZT) transducers with a central frequency of 500 kHz. These transducers allowed simultaneous recording of acoustic emissions, which capture internal defect dynamics, and active ultrasonic pulsing to monitor changes in wave speed and amplitude during deformation. A thin layer of molasses was applied between the sample ends and the

title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
text: leadzirconate-titanate (PZT) transducers with a central frequency of 500 kHz. These transducers allowed simultaneous recording of acoustic emissions, which capture internal defect dynamics, and active ultrasonic pulsing to monitor changes in wave speed and amplitude during deformation. A thin layer of molasses was applied between the sample ends and the loading platens to ensure consistent acoustic coupling. Figure 4 illustrates the strain/loading-controlled loading path. As the macroscopic load increases, micro-fractures initiate, propagate and coalesce within the sample. This internal damage accumulation correlates with a degradation in the measured acoustic velocity (Vp), which we use as an observable proxy for the internal damage state. We explicitly incorporate Vp into the TIP-INN loss function (Eq. 17), constraining the evolution of one ISV to match the observed acoustic trajectory. Figure 4 compares the predicted macroscopic stress and the discovered Helmholtz free energy assuming one, two, or three ISVs. With the acoustic velocity constraint active, increasing the number of ISVs from one to three yields progressively more accurate stress predictions. This indicates that multiple ISVs are necessary to capture the strong nonlinearities induced by progressive fracture damage. The model utilizing three ISVs alongside the acoustic constraint achieves the highest accuracy during the final stages of the experiment, where macroscopic fractures dominate the deformation. title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
text: The model utilizing three ISVs alongside the acoustic constraint achieves the highest accuracy during the final stages of the experiment, where macroscopic fractures dominate the deformation. This demonstrates the utility of multi-modal seismo-acoustic data for regularizing the ill-posed inversion of unobservable ISVs. Furthermore, training TIPINN with the neural ODE integrator remained computationally stable throughout the highly transient experimental data, bypassing the convergence failures frequently encountered with classical Newton-based algorithms under extreme loading. Comparison to a nonlinear viscoelastic baseline To assess the effect of thermodynamic consistency, we compared TIP-INN against a baseline model - a parametric nonlinear visco-elastic Maxwell model with non-linear elasticity and viscosity. We train the model using Bayesian MCMC regression on the same experimental dataset using an identical datadriven protocol (applied strain as input, stress as output). As shown in Supplementary Figure S9, the baseline model fails to reproduce the experimental stress-strain response during both the initial loading and the fracture-dominated final stage of loading. It exhibits systematic deviations unlike TIP-INN, which regularized by thermodynamic constraints and acoustic velocity, maintains high predictive accuracy. This divergence underscores that the strictly convex potential structure and seismo-acoustic regularization within TIP-INN are essential mechanisms. They enable the network to generalize across the full loading history and stably predict macroscopic failure, whereas purely unconstrained empirical fits with phenomenological models have challenges fitting the data and are also highly susceptible to large extrapolation errors. Extracting interpretable physics: Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Potentials (SINP) To overcome the black-box nature of the trained neural potentials, we apply a Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Potentials (SINP) procedure-adapted from the SINDy framework [7]-to distill the learned Helmholtz free energy into closedform analytical expressions. We construct a constrained, strictly convex basis library Оҳ(Оө, Оұ) comprising polynomial and TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks exponential functions, and solve a non-negative least squares (NNLS) regression to identify the parsimonious combination that approximates the neural potential while preserving convexity. This approach works well for simplified analytical potentials, and can recover an exact sparse analytical form with a few terms even if the library has 100s of candidate terms. Figure 4 compares the SINP-calibrated ОЁ against the neural predictions. SINP captures the general trends of the predicted ОЁ, though it struggles to reproduce the detailed fine structure for the most complex (three internal variable) case using the current convex analytical basis. This observation is itself physically informative: it

title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
text: terms. Figure 4 compares the SINP-calibrated ОЁ against the neural predictions. SINP captures the general trends of the predicted ОЁ, though it struggles to reproduce the detailed fine structure for the most complex (three internal variable) case using the current convex analytical basis. This observation is itself physically informative: it indicates that the rheology underlying the experimental data involves non-trivial nonlinear crosscouplings between strain and damage variables that go beyond standard polynomial damage-mechanics potentials. The critical novelty of SINP relative to standard SINDy is that it is applied to the thermodynamic potentials rather than to the dynamics directly, thereby preserving the thermodynamic admissibility achieved by In cases where the SINP-distilled analytical form is sufficiently accurate (see Supplementary Figure S8), it provides a closed-form constitutive law that can be directly implemented in standard finite element codes [2]delivering the computational efficiency of a hard-coded model with the discovery power of machine learning. Discussion In this study, we have presented a novel approach to machine learning constitutive models in complex solids. By strictly employing convex neural networks, the positive, convex properties of both thermodynamic potentials (ОЁ and ОҰвҲ—) are enforced architecturally. This produces more accurate stress predictions and thermodynamic topologies compared to earlier ML approaches (e.g., Masi & Einav [63]), which relied on non-convex networks and consequently predicted physically impossible, negative free energies for simple Maxwell models. Our explicit introduction of the dual dissipation potential rigorously captures the internal driving forces, yielding a thermodynamic framework that captures the coupling of the GSM between the potentials (Xd = вҲ’AОұ). Our numerical results also answer a critical question regarding experimental design: diverse training data spanning multiple frequencies and modes (cyclic, monotonic, and stress-relaxation) samples the potential space more broadly than simple forcing paths. Future rock deformation experiments should deliberately incorporate these diverse loading protocols to adequately map the topology of the underlying potentials and reduce the non-uniqueness of constitutive inversion. In this section, we discuss lessons learned and future directions for the TIP-INN. Number of internal variables for constitutive modeling Our framework clarifies the role of internal state variables (ISVs) in data-driven discovery. As demonstrated by the Burgers model (Section 3.2), increasing the number of ISVs without internal constraints degrades the accuracy of the predicted potentials. In stark contrast, capturing the strong nonlinearities of the experimental rock data (Section 3.3) strictly required multiple ISVs to adequately represent the true internal driving forces. This discrepancy highlights

title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
text: by the Burgers model (Section 3.2), increasing the number of ISVs without internal constraints degrades the accuracy of the predicted potentials. In stark contrast, capturing the strong nonlinearities of the experimental rock data (Section 3.3) strictly required multiple ISVs to adequately represent the true internal driving forces. This discrepancy highlights the necessity of multi-modal data assimilation. By using the acoustic velocity (Vp) as an active constraint on the damage ISV, we provide a physically motivated regularization that anchors the network. This demonstrates that integrating ancillary seismo-acoustic (or other) data may be required to resolve the ill-posed inversion of unobservable microstructural dynamics. We note, however, that mapping ultrasonic dynamic velocities to static deformational ISVs requires careful consideration since velocity can be sensitive to damage (as a proxy - in the form we use here) or could be computed directly as a constraint based on the leastic modulus derived from the free energy potential. Nonetheless, even Vp alone serves as an effective, physically observable regularizer for the damage trajectory and highlights the potential for using other seismo-acoustic features, especially related to the coda waves, as proxies for internal variables. Computational advantages over variational approaches key practical advantage of over incremental variational formulations-such as the Generalized Standard Material Network of Flaschel et al. [33]-is computational efficiency. requires solving a local Newton-Raphson minimization problem at every time step during both training and inference, producing computational costs that scale unfavorably with the number of ISVs and hyperparameters. In our testing of their method, the variational approach is approximately 10 to 100Г— slower per training epoch than TIP-INNвҖҷs neural integrator, which reduces each constitutive update to a single differentiable forward pass. This cost difference is not merely one of wall-clock time: it fundamentally limits the ability to perform the systematic hyperparameter sweeps and architecture searches that are essential when the constitutive model form is unknown a priori. Furthermore, Newton iterations in variational networks are highly prone to convergence failures during the transient, non-monotonic loading paths typical of geological deformation, whereas our Neural ODE formulation exhibits robust convergence across all tested protocols. Interpretability through SINP and general utility The SINP procedure (Section 6.3) provides a reliable pathway from black-box neural potentials to interpretable, closed-form analytical constitutive laws. While the current convex basis library easily captures the general topology of ОЁ, its difficulty in perfectly reproducing the fine structure of the most complex experimental cases reveals genuine, highly

title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
text: utility The SINP procedure (Section 6.3) provides a reliable pathway from black-box neural potentials to interpretable, closed-form analytical constitutive laws. While the current convex basis library easily captures the general topology of ОЁ, its difficulty in perfectly reproducing the fine structure of the most complex experimental cases reveals genuine, highly coupled complexity in the underlying rock rheology. Future work will expand the SINP basis library to include physically motivated functional forms from continuum damage mechanics (e.g., specific straindamage cross-coupling terms) alongside automated model selection criteria. Ultimately, the combination of TIP-INN discovery followed by SINP distillation creates a powerful two-stage pipeline: first, the neural network explores the full nonlinear constitutive state space without rigid modelform assumptions; second, identifies the sparsest analytical expression consistent with the learned physics. This pipeline provides a resolution to the long-standing tension in geomechanics between phenomenological simplicity and physical completeness. Non-smooth constitutive models By replacing classical discrete return-mapping algorithms with a continuous neural integrator, TIP-INN mathematically approximates non-smooth evolution laws (e.g., sharp yield surfaces in rate-independent plasticity) with smooth, continuous functions. In the Prandtl elastoplastic model (Supplementary Section 11.6), TIP-INN naturally smooths the sharp transitions in the Helmholtz free energy as the material evolves from the elastic to the plastic regime. Remarkably, the network discovered a novel, non-linear ISV formulation that accurately represents the macroscopic stress-strain elastoplastic behavior, further illustrating the structural non-uniqueness of macroscopic rheology. In this context, our neural integrator effectively acts as a viscoplastic regularization of rate-independent plasticity. While introducing a Newtonbased discrete solver to could provide more precise representations of sharp yield surfaces (albeit at a significantly higher computational cost), this continuous smoothing is actually a physical advantage for geoscience. As noted in the introduction, the brittle-to-ductile transition in real geological materials-especially when averaged over a macroscopic Representative Elementary Volume (REV)is not a single, sharp mathematical discontinuity. It is a smooth, distributed transition, which is well-represented by the continuous nature of our Neural ODE approach. However, we do note that as macroscopic failure approaches, brittle damage often localizes into discrete shear bands or fracture planes, at which point a REV must be large enough relative to the localized structures to define a meaningful homogenized behavior. Capturing this discrete strain localization remains a fundamental challenge for all continuum-based solvers, including TIP-INN. Gibbs free energy and other thermodynamic potentials While the current framework targets predicting ОЁ and ОҰвҲ— to solve strain-controlled boundaries, its architecture extends

title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
text: enough relative to the localized structures to define a meaningful homogenized behavior. Capturing this discrete strain localization remains a fundamental challenge for all continuum-based solvers, including TIP-INN. Gibbs free energy and other thermodynamic potentials While the current framework targets predicting ОЁ and ОҰвҲ— to solve strain-controlled boundaries, its architecture extends naturally to other thermodynamic potentials, such as the Gibbs free energy and internal energy. The Gibbs free energy switches the control variable from strain to stress via a Legendre transformation of ОЁ (Eq. 13). By deploying the same convex neural network architecture to predict the Gibbs free energy, TIP-INN can easily model stress-controlled experiments. As demonstrated in Supplementary Figure the network successfully predicts an accurate Gibbs free energy topology for the Maxwell model, confirming that any thermodynamic potential requiring strict convexity and positivity can be discovered using this unified computational framework. Broader implications for geoscience applications Because it operates entirely within the general GSM framework, TIP-INN is applicable to any constitutive problem expressible through thermodynamic potentials, including viscoplasticity and coupled thermo-mechanical deformation. The explicit mathematical link between thermodynamic driving forces (Xd) and seismo-acoustic observables established here provides a consistent bridge from laboratory continuum mechanics directly to field-scale seismological monitoring. As observational geophysics increasingly deploys dense sensor networks (e.g., fiber optic based distributed acoustic sensing, large-N nodal arrays) that continuously record micro-seismicity and velocity variations, TIP-INN-derived constitutive laws-constrained by these multi-modal field data-offer a highly robust pathway for upscaling physically interpretable, thermodynamically consistent constitutive models from the laboratory to the crustal scale. Because TIP-INN strictly enforces thermodynamic admissibility rather than merely interpolating data, it provides a mathematically rigorous foundation for the most difficult challenge in geodynamics: extrapolating laboratory laws across the nine-order-of-magnitude gap in strain rates required to model geological time scales. Limitations and future directions We note that the current framework has limitations that invite future development. First, TIP-INN currently does not incorporate formal uncertainty quantification (UQ); the predicted potentials are deterministic point estimates. Future Bayesian extensions вҖ“such as deep ensembles over convex neural networks or Monte Carlo dropout applied to the neural integratorвҖ“ would provide posterior distributions over the learned potentials and successfully propagate epistemic uncertainty to the predicted ISV trajectories [72]. Towards full thermodynamic applicability, incorporating temperature (T ) as an additional state variable is structurally straightforward within the GSM formalism, requiring only temperature-controlled experimental datasets for validation. Similarly, poro-mechanical coupling-where pore fluid pressure modifies the effective stress

title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
text: learned potentials and successfully propagate epistemic uncertainty to the predicted ISV trajectories [72]. Towards full thermodynamic applicability, incorporating temperature (T ) as an additional state variable is structurally straightforward within the GSM formalism, requiring only temperature-controlled experimental datasets for validation. Similarly, poro-mechanical coupling-where pore fluid pressure modifies the effective stress tensor and couples to damagedriven permeability evolution [24]-represents a critical extension for modeling fluid-saturated crustal rocks, fault zone hydrology, and reservoir geomechanics. In the future, the present 1D uniaxial formulation can be extended to full 3D tensorial constitutive discovery to address field-scale geomechanical problems. This extension is architecturally tractable within the TIP-INN framework, requiring careful enforcement of frame indifference and material symmetry constraints on the convex neural network inputs to account for both inherent structural anisotropy (e.g., foliations) and damage-induced anisotropy. However, the practical utility of this extension hinges critically on the availability of concurrent multi-axial stress-strain data [e.g. 93, 94]. Using multi-axial loading platforms capable of simultaneously resolving independent stress and strain components is therefore a prerequisite for the 3D extension of TIP-INN to realistic geomechanical settings. Conclusion We introduced the TIP-INN, a thermodynamically consistent neural network framework for constitutive discovery in geophysics. The network is trained exclusively on macroscopic stress-strain data pairs, to discover underlying Helmholtz free energy (ОЁ) and dual dissipation (ОҰвҲ—) potentials, which are known for our forward model data, but not for experimental data. With limited data, TIP-INN successfully reconstructs the topologies of ОЁ and ОҰвҲ—simply by enforcing the non-negative mechanical dissipation and constitutive ODEs derived from these potentials within the Generalized Standard Materials formalism through architectural construction rather than soft penalties. This ensures physical consistency across elastic, plastic, and viscous regimes, eliminating the possibility of thermodynamic violations through the constitutive laws. To resolve the ill-posed inversion of unobservable internal state variables, TIP-INN directly assimilates laboratory seismoacoustic data into the objective function, explicitly linking sub-grid defect dynamics to macroscopic continuum mechanics. Subsequently, the SINP procedure distills the trained highdimensional neural potentials into closed-form analytical equations to recover explicit, interpretable constitutive laws. By providing a flexible, data-driven, and physically rigorous framework, presents a new path for bridging disciplinary gaps across geophysics and geodynamics. TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks Materials and methods The Generalized Standard Materials formalism In many geophysical problems, the infinitesimal strain approximation is a valid approach and significantly simplifies the computational implementation [68, 84, 38]. In the GSM, the states are uniquely defined by the infinitesimal total strain, Оө, and internal state variables, Оұ. Additional thermodynamic state variables, such as temperature, T , and fluid pressure, pf, can be incorporated for more complex systems [18, 16]. In general, Оө is an observable physical quantity in lab experiments or field data. In contrast, Оұ, which encompasses irreversible phenomena such as damage, fracture density, and visco-plastic strain, cannot be observed directly. In some cases, seismo-acoustic or proxies can help constrain internal structure evolution. To ensure thermodynamic consistency in GSM, the material response (constitutive model) of the system is represented through a set of two scalar thermodynamic potentials with respect to the state variables: Helmholtz free energy, ОЁ, and dissipation potential, ОҰ [66, 39, 19, 29, 35, 46, 17], which are extension of the linear Onsager thermodynamics framework [70, 71, 28, 95, 96]. The key properties of the two scalar potentials are: 1.Helmholtz Free Energy, ОЁ(Оөij, Оұk, T ) : Defines the storage of energy (reversible) and can be defined as per unit volume (Energy Density - J/m3) or per unit mass (Specific Energy - J/kg). We use the per unit volume notation for the rest of the text. In general, the Helmholtz free energy, ОЁ, of a system is determined by the total strains, Оөij, internal state variables, Оұk, and temperature, T . For problems under isothermal conditions, ОЁ, only associated to Оө = Оөij and Оұ = Оұk, is smooth, non-negative and convex with respect to Оө and Оұ and ОЁ = 0 at the origin, where Оө = 0 and Оұ = 0. We use the per unit volume notation for the rest of the text. The dissipation potential ОҰ is determined

The dissipation potential ОҰ is determined by the rates of total strain, ЛҷОөij, rates of internal state variables, ЛҷОұk, heat flux, q, internal state variables, Оұk, and temperature, T . Analogously, for isothermal problems, ОҰ, only associated with ЛҷОө and ЛҷОұ, is non-negative and convex with respect to ЛҷОө and ЛҷОұ and ОҰ = 0 at the origin, where ЛҷОө = 0 and ЛҷОұ = 0, namely, ОҰ(ЛҷОө, ЛҷОұ) вүҘ0 , вҲӮ2ОҰ(ЛҷОө, ЛҷОұ) вҲӮЛҷОө2 вүҘ0 , вҲӮ2ОҰ(ЛҷОө, ЛҷОұ) вҲӮЛҷОұ2 вүҘ0 , ОҰ(0, 0) = 0 , вҲӮЛҷОө = 0 , вҲӮЛҷОұ = 0 . Compared with the Helmholtz free energy, the dissipation potential could be locally linear and non-smooth, for example, in rate-independent elastoplasticity [87]. These constraints on ОЁ and ОҰ ensure that the general response of a standard material is always stable and thermodynamically consistent. Having specified the thermodynamic potentials, the constitutive model must satisfy the 2nd law of thermodynamics. This requirement is expressed through the Clausius-Duhem Inequality derived from the entropy and energy conservation equations, which restricts admissible constitutive relations and ensures non-negative mechanical dissipation [91, 20]: D = Пғ : ЛҷОө вҲ’ПҒ( ЛҷОЁ + s ЛҷT ) вҲ’q В· вҲҮT вүҘ0 , (3) where D is the dissipation rate, Пғ the stress, ПҒ the density, and s is the entropy. (8) Eq. 8 describes the relations between irreversible phenomena and thermodynamic forces (Biot-Rayleigh equation). Here, Xd is the thermodynamic force driving microstructural internal variable evolution and AОұ is its conjugate and called the thermodynamic Affinity. Overall, XОұ provides the direct link between kinetics and thermodynamics. To contextualize these forces physically, consider a Maxwell model. In this framework, the internal state variables Оұ represent the hidden internal viscous strains of the constituent dashpots. The reversible force, AОұ, represents the energetic restoring stress accumulated in the adjacent elastic springs resulting from that internal deformation. Conversely, the dissipative force, Xd, corresponds directly to the viscous drag stress generated within the dashpot itself, which resists the rate of internal deformation ( ЛҷОұ). The required thermodynamic equality Xd = вҲ’AОұ fundamentally enforces internal equilibrium ensuring that the elastic restoring forces are perfectly balanced by the viscous resisting forces at all times. Substituting Eq. 6 into Eq. 8, the constitutive relations can be expressed in terms of ОЁ and ОҰ as: Пғ = вҲӮОЁ вҲӮОө + вҲӮОҰ вҲӮЛҷОө , вҲӮОұ + вҲӮОҰ вҲӮЛҷОұ . Then the constitutive relations described by Eq. 10 can be further simplified to Пғ = вҲӮОЁ вҲӮОө , вҲӮОұ + вҲӮОҰ вҲӮЛҷОұ . (11) By applying a Legendre-Fenchel transformation to the dissipation potential ОҰ, we obtain a dual dissipation potential, ОҰвҲ—[39, 46] as ОҰвҲ—(Xd) = sup ЛҷОұ  Xd вҲ’ОҰ( ЛҷОұ)  , with ЛҷОұ = вҲӮОҰвҲ— вҲӮXd . (12) The above equation describes an evolution process for the internal variable and is thus often more useful than the state laws of Eq. 8. Similar to the properties of ОҰ, is non-negative and convex due to the Legendre-Fenchel transformation property. Notice that Eq. (2nd law of thermodynamics) is automatically satisfied if ОҰ and ОҰвҲ—are convex, non-negative, and zero at the origin. Although our primary analysis in this study uses the Helmholtz free energy and dual dissipation, it is straightforward to use the GSM theory with other thermodynamic potentials, such as the Gibbs Free Energy, Internal Energy, and Enthalpy. Typically, ОЁ serves as the central pivot for these transformations since it naturally couples the thermal intensive variable (T ) with the mechanical extensive variable (Оө), which are both lab/field measurable quantities. For instance, we can switch control variables from Оө (strained-controlled) to Пғ (stress-controlled), using the Legendre transformation to obtain the Gibbs free energy (G) of the system: G(Пғ, Оұ, T ) = sup Пө (ОЁ вҲ’Пғ : Оө) (13) Analogously, the strains and driving forces can be derived from G as Оө = вҲӮG/вҲӮПғ and Xd = вҲӮG/вҲӮОұ, respectively. The key advantage of working directly with thermodynamic potentials, instead of stress-strain relationships is that the material response of a generalized solid relies heavily on the local curvature of the thermodynamic potentials. For instance, the Hessian of ОЁ, L = вҲӮ2ОЁ(M)/вҲӮM2 (where M refers to the relevant state variables), encodes the fundamental reversible stiffness properties of the material [11, 79, 88]. Specifically, the second-order derivative of ОЁ with respect to strain, C = вҲӮ2ОЁ/вҲӮОө2, gives the isothermal elastic moduli. At present, a key challenge with using GSM for constitutive modeling with geophysical applications lies in learning the appropriate forms of the potentials (ОЁ and ОҰвҲ—) from lab and field datasets, which can then be used

title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
text: of ОЁ with respect to strain, C = вҲӮ2ОЁ/вҲӮОө2, gives the isothermal elastic moduli. At present, a key challenge with using GSM for constitutive modeling with geophysical applications lies in learning the appropriate forms of the potentials (ОЁ and ОҰвҲ—) from lab and field datasets, which can then be used for continuum models. Machine learning algorithms are now widely used for constitutive modeling due to their computational efficiency and adaptability for nonlinear models [60, 73, 8, 5, 49, 40, 36, 67]. This strongly motivates our approach, outlined in the next section, to develop a neural network framework for learning the GSM potentials directly from the strain-stress time series data. Thermodynamics of Irreversible Processes Informed Neural Network (TIP-INN) In many relevant experiments for geological materials, the rates of state variables and the full state of materials are not known at each time step. Thus, the time-evolution process for state variables is inherently non-integrable with classical numerical methods without assuming a constitutive model and/or thermodynamic potentials. In contrast, a neural integrator (using a neural network learned integration function) only requires partial and sparse material states for time evolution since the state variables can be predicted by minimizing the error between the predicted trajectories and the experimental measurements. Therefore, the neural integrator generally solves the evolution equations more efficiently than an implicit numerical method (i.e., Newton method). The idea of embedding the neural integrator for state variablesвҖҷ evolution has been investigated for constitutive modeling in many different works [63, 59, 50, 89, 33, 80, 44, 51]. These works focus primarily on the stress-strain relationship, with limited attention to the predicted thermodynamic potentials (e.g., Helmholtz free energy and dual dissipation). Compared to the numerical ordinary differential equation (ODE) solver, the neural integrator allows the parametrization of continuous-time dynamics of state variables: Лҷy = f Оё(y(t), p), y(0) = y0, t вҲҲ[0, te] , (14) where p is the set of control variables that the state variables depend on and f Оё is the evolution equation (ODE) parametrized by a feed-forward neural network with Оё representing the weights and bias terms. Considering an initial condition y0 at t = 0 and integrating Eq. 14 from 0 to any time t yields the state variable y(t) at t yОё(t) = y0 + Z t f Оё(y(П„), p)dП„ . (15) During the learning process, Оё is determined by a gradient descent algorithm to minimize the loss function

title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
text: y0 at t = 0 and integrating Eq. 14 from 0 to any time t yields the state variable y(t) at t yОё(t) = y0 + Z t f Оё(y(П„), p)dП„ . (15) During the learning process, Оё is determined by a gradient descent algorithm to minimize the loss function n=1  yОё(n) вҲ’y(n) 2 , (16) where we sum the errors between the neural-network predictions, yОё(n), and the experimental measurements, y(n), at a series of discrete time points tn with n = 1, 2, . . . , N and tN = te. We propose a general parametric neural network (Thermodynamics of Irreversible Processes Informed Neural Network, TIP-INN) for constitutive modeling by learning the thermodynamic potentials of Helmholtz free energy, ОЁ and dual dissipation, modified after the neural integrator in [63] and [33]. Figure illustrates the detailed structures of which involves a neural integrator to solve the evolution equation and convex neural networks to predict ОЁ and ОҰвҲ—. In particular, we use a modular forward solver based on the Generalized Standard Materials (GSM) framework that derives system dynamics directly from fundamental thermodynamic potentials, bypassing the need to manually derive and integrate specific constitutive ordinary differential equations (ODEs) for each new rheology. In this architecture, we have the definitions for the scalar ОЁ and ОҰвҲ—. At each time step, an automatic differentiation engine evaluates the gradients of these potentials with respect to the state variables (вҲҮОЁ, вҲҮОҰвҲ—). This TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks operation dynamically computes the thermodynamic driving forces, such as reversible stresses and thermodynamic affinities, as well as the evolution rates of internal state variables (e.g., viscous strains and damage parameters). The resulting auto-derived equations are then integrated using a stiff ODE solver with adaptive time-stepping. The primary advantage of this potential-driven approach is extreme structural flexibility; transitioning between disparate material behaviors, ranging from simple thermoelasticity to complex thermo-elasto-viscous models with non-linear flow laws requires modifying only the input scalar potentials, leaving the core solver mechanics completely unchanged. Furthermore, the framework naturally accommodates both strain and stress-controlled boundary conditions (employing local Legendre transforms at each time step for the stress-forced case) and rigorously tracks fully coupled temperature evolution by incorporating mechanical dissipation and specific heat capacity derived from the Hessian of the free energy. To enforce the convex and positive properties of ОЁ and ОҰвҲ—for constitutive modeling, we follow the concept

title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
text: Legendre transforms at each time step for the stress-forced case) and rigorously tracks fully coupled temperature evolution by incorporating mechanical dissipation and specific heat capacity derived from the Hessian of the free energy. To enforce the convex and positive properties of ОЁ and ОҰвҲ—for constitutive modeling, we follow the concept of convex neural network with skip connections proposed by Amos et al. [3]. By constraining weight parameters in the network to be non-negative and choosing convex and non-decreasing activation functions, the output of the network is guaranteed to be convex with respect to the input. With such a strategy, the convexity of ОЁ with respect to Пө and Оұ is preserved, as well as ОҰвҲ—with respect to Xd. Figure 1 shows the detailed structures of a convex neural network. Choosing convex activation functions is straightforward. Specifically, we adopt a quadratic activation function to model viscoelastic materials (Maxwell and Burgers). The smoothed absolute activation function is chosen to model the experimental data with fracture propagation as sharp transitions are observed on the stress-strain relationships. The enforcement of non-negative weighting parameters in the network is achieved by setting w+ i = g(wi) where g : R 7вҶ’R+ is an auxiliary function that maps real numbers to non-negative real numbers. The convex neural networks for both ОЁ and ОҰвҲ— have very similar structures, except for the different inputs (see Figure 1). Given the data regime and the framework depicted in Figure 1, the learning process of TIP-INN consists of minimizing the following loss function LTIPвҲ’INN = LПғ + LD + LОЁ+ + LОҰвҲ— + + LПғ(0) + LОЁ(0) + LОҰвҲ—(0) + LL2 . (17) LПғ is the loss on the stress; and LОҰвҲ— + fulfill the positivity of the intrinsic dissipation rate, Helmholtz free energy and dual dissipation, respectively. LПғ(0), LОЁ(0) and LОҰвҲ—(0) enforce the initial conditions for the stress, Helmholtz free energy and dual dissipation, respectively. To avoid data overfitting, an L2-regularization on the weighting parameters (LL2) with a small penalty parameter 10вҲ’5 is added to the loss function. Note that different weighting parameters could be considered to control the importance of different terms in Eq. 17 although equal weights are used in this work. The specific definitions of each term are as follows: LПғ = 1 n=1  ПғОё(n) вҲ’Пғ(n) 2 , n=1 вҲ’DОё(n) , n=1 вҲ’ОЁОё(n) , + = 1 n=1 Оё(n) , LПғ(0) =  ПғОё(n = 1; t

text: different terms in Eq. 17 although equal weights are used in this work. The specific definitions of each term are as follows: LПғ = 1 n=1  ПғОё(n) вҲ’Пғ(n) 2 , n=1 вҲ’DОё(n) , n=1 вҲ’ОЁОё(n) , + = 1 n=1 Оё(n) , LПғ(0) =  ПғОё(n = 1; t = 0) 2 ,  ОЁОё(n = 1; t = 0) 2 ,  Оё(n = 1; t = 0) 2 , Nw Nw n=1  wi 2 , where вҹЁxвҹ©= (|x| + x)/2 is the Macaulay bracket and Nw denotes the number of weighting parameters in the neural network. Note that the sampling frequencies for each term in the loss function (Eq. 17) can be adjusted by the user to reduce the computational cost of training. For example, adding more sample points for the evolution of state variables could improve the accuracy of the predictions. In this work, the full data points are considered in LПғ, LD, LОЁ+ and LОҰвҲ— +. An early stopping criterion during training is used to avoid overfitting. Additional training details and hyperparameter settings are provided in Supplementary Section 11.2. A key difference in our approach compared to existing variational methods (e.g., the GSMN of [33]) is the use of a neural integrator rather than a local optimization for the evolution of internal variables ( ЛҷОұ). In the incremental variational formulation, the ODE is not solved directly; instead, a local minimization problem is solved at every time step tn+1: (Пөn+1, Оұn+1) = arg min Пө,Оұ (ОЁ(Пө, Оұ) + вҲҶtОҰвҲ—  Оұ вҲ’Оұn вҲҶt  вҲ’Пғ : Пө) Replacing the numerical solver (or Newton-Raphson iteration) with a neural integrator, the training process is significantly faster and more robust as long as the time steps are sufficiently small, since the time evolution reduces to a single forward pass. Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Potentials (SINP) The objective of this framework is to extract a strictly convex, analytically exact Free Energy Functional V (Пө, Оұ) from discrete state-space data. Here, Пө вҲҲRdПө represents observable kinematic states (e.g., strain), and Оұ вҲҲR+ dОұ denotes internal latent state variables mapping irreversible dissipation (e.g., damage, defect density). Standard sparse identification architectures, such as the Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics (SINDy) [7], predominantly utilize unconstrained Sequentially Thresholded Least Squares (STLSQ) or L1-regularized regression (Lasso) [90]. These algorithms are structurally deficient for thermodynamic potential identification for two primary reasons: L1 penalties introduce amplitude shrinkage

title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
text: dissipation (e.g., damage, defect density). Standard sparse identification architectures, such as the Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Dynamics (SINDy) [7], predominantly utilize unconstrained Sequentially Thresholded Least Squares (STLSQ) or L1-regularized regression (Lasso) [90]. These algorithms are structurally deficient for thermodynamic potential identification for two primary reasons: L1 penalties introduce amplitude shrinkage [90, 31] that violates exact energy equivalence, and unconstrained algorithms suffer catastrophic collinear instability over highly resolved activation grids. To resolve this, we present an overcomplete, physicsinformed basis projection coupled with a Dual-Manifold Scaled Non-Negative Least Squares (NNLS) active-set solver [56]. The Convex Overcomplete Basis and Reference State We map the phase space x = [ПөT , ОұT ]T into a high-dimensional dictionary Оҳ(x) composed strictly of positive semi-definite basis functions. The functional is expressed as: V (x) = k ОІkОёk(x), subject to ОІk вүҘ0 вҲҖk (19) By strictly enforcing 2p вүҘ for all polynomial orders and omitting an intercept parameter (ОІ0 = 0), we ensure that Оёk(0) = 0. This rigorously satisfies the fundamental thermodynamic reference state V (0) = 0; an undeformed, virgin material possesses zero stored energy. The dictionary Оҳ isolates the system into distinct energetic sub-manifolds: I. Isolated Kinematics and Internal Storage The pure elastic strain energy and isolated internal defect energy are mapped as: Оёkin вҲҲ  вҹЁПөiвҹ©2p + , вҹЁвҲ’Пөiвҹ©2p +  , Оёiso вҲҲ  Оұ2p i  (20) Because the physical definitions of the internal variables restrict them to the positive orthant (Оұi вүҘ0), the computationally expensive Macaulay bracket вҹЁВ·вҹ©+ is mathematically redundant for Оёiso and is bypassed to accelerate tensor operations. II. Coupled Dissipative Manifolds The interaction between kinematics and the internal state is governed by discrete activation thresholds. These define the exact yield surfaces where the kinematic driving force exceeds the resistance of the internal defect network: Оёcpl вҲҲ  вҹЁВұПөi вҲ’cmОұjвҹ©2p +  (21) where cm вҲҲCgrid operates on a highly resolved, overcomplete discrete grid (e.g., [0.1, 3.0]). The Macaulay bracket ensures C0 continuity across the phase boundary, maintaining a dormant state until the precise local driving force threshold is breached. Latent Interaction Topologies: Smooth vs. Piecewise Interactions between competing internal mechanisms (e.g., distinct dislocation slip systems) require specific topological assumptions based on the underlying physics. We define two distinct bases to map the latent sub-manifold: 1.Bracketed (Piecewise Directional): Assumes strict, asymmetric thresholds where one mechanism permanently pins or dominates another. Mapped via вҹЁОұi вҲ’cmОұjвҹ©2p + , energy is

text: competing internal mechanisms (e.g., distinct dislocation slip systems) require specific topological assumptions based on the underlying physics. We define two distinct bases to map the latent sub-manifold: 1.Bracketed (Piecewise Directional): Assumes strict, asymmetric thresholds where one mechanism permanently pins or dominates another. Mapped via вҹЁОұi вҲ’cmОұjвҹ©2p + , energy is exclusively stored if Оұi > cmОұj. This generates a C0 continuous, piecewise phase space representing definitive microstructural barriers. 2.Unbracketed (Smooth Symmetric): Assumes continuous, mutual energy exchange without discrete activation barriers. Mapped via symmetric polynomials (Оұi Вұ Оұj)2p. Because the exponent 2p is inherently even, the manifold remains globally convex and continuous. This topology maps smooth, symmetric restorative thermodynamic forces between coupled latent fields. We also include some additional non-linear terms like (Оұi вҲ—Оұj)2p. in the basis function. Exact Sparse Recovery via Active-Set NNLS To identify the sparse set of active thermodynamic mechanisms, standard L1 optimization solves min вҲҘОҳОІ вҲ’yвҲҘ2 2 + О»вҲҘОІвҲҘ1 [90]. However, this subjects the identified stiffness parameters to an unphysical shrinkage bias (ОІL1 = |ОІtrue| вҲ’О») [31], destroying exact energy conservation. Furthermore, unconstrained regression on the highly collinear grid Cgrid results in massive, oscillating positive and negative coefficients as the solver attempts to cancel localized noise. Thus, we do not use algebraic penalty regularizers for our analysis and reframe the identification as a Constrained Convex Optimization problem utilizing NNLS. To stabilize the activeset selection against the extreme ill-conditioning inherent in the overcomplete grid Cgrid, we perform a bijective mapping of the dictionary onto an LвҲһunit hypercube. We define the diagonal scaling matrix S вҲҲRKГ—K where Skk = вҲҘОёkвҲҘвҲһ, and a target scale sy = вҲҘyвҲҘвҲһ. We then solve the dimensionless optimization: min ЛҶОІ (ОҳSвҲ’1)ЛҶОІ вҲ’sвҲ’1 y y subject to ЛҶОІk вүҘ0 вҲҖk (22) where ЛҶОІ is the scaled parameter vector. This condition ensures that every basis function Оёk contributes with O(1) weight to the initial gradient calculation, preventing the solver from prematurely pinning low-amplitude but physically correct mechanisms to the zero-boundary. The strict non-negativity constraint forms a geometric boundary that fundamentally neutralizes collinear instability; the solver is mathematically forbidden from utilizing negative amplitudes to cancel redundant grid terms. The success of this approach is dictated by the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) Complementary condition [6] at the optimal solution ЛҶОІвҲ—: ЛҶОІвҲ— k В· gвҲ— k = 0 вҲҖk (23) where gвҲ— k is the objective gradient. This condition mandates a strict bifurcation of the overcomplete dictionary. If a feature is

title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
text: terms. The success of this approach is dictated by the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) Complementary condition [6] at the optimal solution ЛҶОІвҲ—: ЛҶОІвҲ— k В· gвҲ— k = 0 вҲҖk (23) where gвҲ— k is the objective gradient. This condition mandates a strict bifurcation of the overcomplete dictionary. If a feature is inactive (spurious or collinear), it is pinned to the boundary ( ЛҶОІвҲ— k = 0) with a positive gradient pointing outward. If a feature is active ( ЛҶОІвҲ— k > 0), its local gradient must be exactly zero (gвҲ— k = 0). Once the optimal scaled vector ЛҶОІвҲ—is identified via the KKTconstrained active-set projection, the exact physical stiffness parameters are recovered via the inverse mapping: ОІвҲ—= sySвҲ’1 ЛҶОІвҲ— (24) Because this scaling is a purely linear transformation and avoids objective-function penalties (e.g., О»вҲҘОІвҲҘ1), it preserves the exact unbiased local minimum. This "Dual-Manifold" approach combines the numerical stability of bounded optimization with the theoretical exactitude of Ordinary Least Squares strictly on the recovered physical support manifold. A standard numerical threshold filter is applied post-recovery to eliminate machineprecision floating-point artifacts, yielding exact L0 sparsity. Since the gradient is precisely zero for all active terms, the NNLS active-set algorithm operates equivalent to unconstrained Ordinary Least Squares strictly on the true physical support manifold. This isolates the L0 unpenalized minimum, recovering the exact theoretical amplitude (e.g., retrieving a stiffness of exactly 250.0) while enforcing global convexity and unconditional thermodynamic stability. The SINP basis library employed here captures the general topology of the learned potentials but sometimes struggles with complex cross-coupling terms in the most nonlinear cases; future work will expand the library to include physically motivated functional forms from continuum damage mechanics (e.g., (1 вҲ’ Оұ)nОө2 coupling terms) [13, 14, 57].
