TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks Supplementary Material Rheological representations Phenomenologically, the overall mechanical behavior of any materials, in 1D, can be modeled by arbitrary serial and parallel arrangements of a few elementary (non)-linear constitutive model elements elastic springs, viscous dashpots, and frictional sliders, representing the elastic, viscous, and plastic processes, respectively [87]. Figure S1 shows the rheological representations for Maxwell and Burgers viscoelastic models. The formulas of Helmholtz free energy for Maxwell and Burgers models are ОЁ = 2 EvПө2 e and ОЁ = 2 Ev1Пө2 e1 + 2 Ev2Пө2 e2, respectively. The formulas of dual dissipation for Maxwell and Burgers models are ОҰвҲ—= 2О·v Пғ2 v and ОҰвҲ—= 2О·v1 Пғ2 v1 + 2О·v2 Пғ2 v2, respectively. рқ‘¬рқ’— рқңјрқ’— рқ‘¬рқ’—рқҹҸ рқ‘¬рқ’—рқҹҗ рқңјрқ’—рқҹҗ рқңјрқ’—рқҹҸ Maxwell Burgers Fig. S1. Rheological representations of the Maxwell viscoelastic model with a single internal variable (associated with О·v), and Burgers viscoelastic model with two internal variables (associated with О·v1 and О·v2 ). TIP-INN training details In all test cases, the learning rate was set to 10вҲ’3, and the training was allowed to progress for 20,000 epochs with an early stopping criterion to avoid overfitting. In TIP-INN, the convex neural network architecture consists of an input layer with a linear activation function, followed by a single hidden layer. The hidden layer contains multiple fully connected neurons for the Maxwell and Burgers models, and 20 fully connected neurons for the experimental datasets, and is used to learn the Helmholtz free energy (ОЁ) and dual dissipation ОҰвҲ—. The output layer with a linear activation function contains a single neuron, consistent with the scalar nature of ОЁ and ОҰвҲ—. As described

title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
text: Maxwell and Burgers models, and 20 fully connected neurons for the experimental datasets, and is used to learn the Helmholtz free energy (ОЁ) and dual dissipation ОҰвҲ—. The output layer with a linear activation function contains a single neuron, consistent with the scalar nature of ОЁ and ОҰвҲ—. As described in Section 6, a quadratic activation function is used for modeling Maxwell and Burgers viscoelastic materials, while a smoothed absolute activation function is employed for experimental data. Our numerical tests indicate that a single hidden layer with 10-30 neurons is generally sufficient to capture a wide range of constitutive behaviors from both analytical and experimental datasets. Increasing the hidden layer depth does not improve the accuracy of the predicted thermodynamic potentials. In fact, introducing multiple hidden layers (e.g., > 4) can lead to training instability, as the quadratic activation function amplifies weights multiplicatively across layers, resulting in divergence. TIP-INN to model Maxwell viscoelastic materials Figure S2 shows the predicted stress for different types of loads by training TIP-INN with a single load. Notice that accurate stresses were predicted in nearly all cases. Figure S3 presents the predicted stress and Helmholtz free energy obtained by TIP-INN on the training dataset for the Maxwell viscoelastic model. The results are consistent with those observed for the testing data discussed in Section 3.1, leading to similar conclusions regarding the accuracy and robustness of TIP-INN. In addition, Figure S3 shows that the training and testing datasets exhibit distinct frequency characteristics, as revealed by the power spectral analysis. In this study, the Lomb-Scargle periodogram is employed to evaluate the power spectral density for frequencies below 10 Hz. TIP-INN to model Burgers viscoelastic materials Figure S4 shows the predicted viscosity and YoungвҖҷs modulus for on the testing data considering a Burgers viscoelastic model. Notice that Burgers model can be effectively approximated by the Maxwell model with a nonlinear YoungвҖҷs modulus (left column). Moreover, the same macroscopic stressstrain response for Burgers model can be achieved through different combinations of internal linear/nonlinear material properties. Thus, the predicted material properties are observed to be significantly different from the true solutions. The cyclic and monotonically oscillatory loads used to train TIP-INN are plotted in Figure S5, along with the predicted stress, Helmholtz free energy and internal variables. The training results lead to conclusions consistent with those obtained from the testing data (see Section 3.2). In the case with two internal variables,

Gibbs free energy In this work, we consider the sign-flipped Gibbs free energy, that is, G+(Пғ, Оұ) = вҲ’G(Пғ, Оұ) (see Eq. 13), to model isothermal problems. G+ is non-negative and convex with respect to Пғ and Оұ. Therefore, the same neural networks used to predict the Helmholtz free energy and dual dissipation in TIPINN can be applied to predict G+ here. Figure S6 shows that accurate G+ are predicted by the convex neural network for the Maxwell viscoelastic model. This numerical example confirms that different types of thermodynamic potentials can be learned using the same computational frameworks that were proposed in this work. A single internal variable is employed in TIPINN. Figure S7 shows the predicted stress and Helmholtz free energy. While the stress is accurately captured, large errors are observed in the predicted Helmholtz free energy. As the material response evolves from elasticity to plasticity, TIPINN struggles to capture the sharp transition. Increasing the network depth or modifying the activation functions does not significantly improve the accuracy of the predicted Helmholtz free energy. This limitation stems from the inability of the neural integrator to capture the yield surface effectively. Replacing the neural integrator with a Newton-based returnmapping algorithm yields accurate predictions of the yield surface [33], but with a much higher computational cost. Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Potentials (SINP) To overcome the black-box nature of the trained neural potentials, we apply a Sparse Identification of Nonlinear Potentials (SINP) procedure-adapted from the SINP framework [7]-to distill the learned Helmholtz free energy, ОЁ, into closedform analytical expressions that can be directly imported into continuum geodynamic codes. Figure S8 shows the SINPcalibrated ОЁ against the neural predictions. Notice that an exact quadratic ОЁ function (Truth: ОЁ = Оө2 e = (Оө вҲ’Оұ1)2) was discovered for the Maxwell model. It is also observed that the convex and positive properties of ОЁ were also maintained by the discovered formulas for other models. Fitting the data model

Non-Linear Viscoelastic Model Formulation To capture the time-dependent deformation observed in the Pacaya basaltic lava flows [23], we employ a non-linear viscoelastic framework that extends the generalized Maxwell model [75]. Unlike standard subsurface models that assume static material properties, this approach defines the elastic moduli (E) and viscosities (О·) as evolving functions of the internal state, represented here by the cumulative axial strain (Пө). As seen in the experimental mechanical data, the samples exhibit significant non-linearity and creep that require this dynamic parameterization. The total stress Пғ at any time t is calculated using a recursive direct-fit method [75], which utilizes a history state variable to bypass the computational cost of storing the full strain history and allow fast MCMC inversion. The governing equations for the strain-dependent case are: Пғ(Пө, t) = n i=1 Ei(Пө) dПөi dt dt + n i=1 ( hi[Пө(t), t]eвҲ’вҲҶt/П„i + Ei(Пө)(1 вҲ’eвҲ’вҲҶt/П„i) вҲҶt/П„i dПөi dt dt ) (2) Where the history variable hi represents the viscoelastic state at the previous increment: hi[Пө(t), t]eвҲ’вҲҶt/П„i = Z t n Ei(Пө)eвҲ’(tвҲ’вҲҶt+П„)/П„io dПөi dt dt (3) Evolution of Material Potentials The model accounts for damage-induced degradation, such as pore collapse and microcracking. The long-term elastic modulus is held constant as E = a, while each of the viscoelastic moduli Ei (for the elastic element outside the Maxwell element as well as an internal visco-elastic element) and viscosities О·i evolve according to power-law relations based on normalized strain: Ei(Пө) = a  1 + b  Пө Пөmax c (4) О·i(Пө, t) = d 1 + e  Пө Пөmax f! (5) The relaxation time П„i is self-consistently derived from these evolving properties: П„i(О·i, Ei, t) = 10О·i(Пө) Ei(Пө) (6) Bayesian Parameter Identification (MCMC) To resolve the structural non-uniqueness inherent in rock rheology where multiple parameter combinations can yield similar stress-strain curves and also to prevent robustness against local minima, we utilize a two-stage optimization and inference pipeline. Stage 1: Global Optimization. We maximize a log-likelihood function (log L) that quantifies the residuals between experimental stress Пғdata and model predictions Пғmodel: log L(Пғ) = вҲ’1 n i=1 (Пғdata(ti) вҲ’Пғmodel(ti; Оё))2 (7) The parameter space Оё is sampled using the Tree-structured Parzen Estimator (TPE) algorithm via the Optuna package [1]. Stage 2: Posterior Estimation. The optimal parameters from Stage 1 initialize a Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampler using the emcee package [34]. This yields the corner plots (Fig. 3c, d) used to estimate the full posterior probability density function (PDF), ensuring that parameters are wellconstrained and physically consistent. Comparison to Standard Subsurface Models Typical geodynamic models often rely on linear elastic assumptions that fail to capture the irreversible deformation TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks and creep observed in volcanic basalts [83]. Our results for the Pacaya core and base samples (p5914) demonstrate that elastic moduli can increase by factors as high as 100s as the material compacts or damages [23]. The integration of Bayesian MCMC allows us to quantify the uncertainty in these transitions, identifying the precise moment the material shifts from purely elastic to complex viscoelastic regimes. However, even with the non-linearity, the model has strong challenges with fully representing the stress data. Fig. S9. Experimental stress data was compared to the three different forward models

Rate-State Friction in GSM Theory This note provides a rigorous thermodynamic mapping of the empirical Dieterich-Ruina Rate-and-State Friction (RSF) equations [82, 62, 76, 61, 77] into the framework of Generalized Solid Mechanics (GSM). We demonstrate that the empirical RSF laws violate the integrability conditions required for a single dissipation potential, necessitating a bipotential (nonassociated) formulation [26, 25] with two independent pseudopotentials: one for slip velocity and one for state evolution. By decoupling the mechanical and configurational dissipative mechanisms, we derive both the Aging and Slip laws exactly while guaranteeing strict adherence to the Clausius-Duhem inequality. Kinematics and State Variables We consider a representative elementary area of a fault interface. The total displacement jump Оҙ is additively decomposed into an elastic component Оҙe and an inelastic (plastic) slip component Оҙp: Оҙ = Оҙe + Оҙp (8) The thermodynamic state is described by q = (Оҙe, Оё), where Оё [s] is the internal state variable representing the average contact lifetime of the asperity population. The Helmholtz Free Energy (ОЁ) The reversible energy storage is governed by the Helmholtz free energy density: ОЁ(Оҙe, Оё) = 1 2 k(Оҙe)2 + U(Оё) (9) where k is the effective elastic stiffness and U(Оё) is the internal surface energy density. Consistent with the logarithmic RSF framework, we adopt: U(Оё) = вҲ’bПғnDc ln  Оё Оё0  (10) yielding the thermodynamic forces: П„ = вҲӮОЁ вҲӮОҙe = kОҙe (11) AОё = вҲ’вҲӮОЁ вҲӮОё = bПғnDc Оё (12) Here П„ is the macroscopic

text: stiffness and U(Оё) is the internal surface energy density. Consistent with the logarithmic RSF framework, we adopt: U(Оё) = вҲ’bПғnDc ln  Оё Оё0  (10) yielding the thermodynamic forces: П„ = вҲӮОЁ вҲӮОҙe = kОҙe (11) AОё = вҲ’вҲӮОЁ вҲӮОё = bПғnDc Оё (12) Here П„ is the macroscopic shear stress and AОё is the state affinity, representing the configurational force driving contact maturation. Note that AОё > 0 for all admissible Оё > 0 and is O(1), scaling with the state evolution parameter b. The Bipotential Framework (Non-Associated Dissipation) The empirical equations cannot be derived from a single dissipation potential ОҰвҲ—(П„, AОё) because they violate the integrability (Maxwell) condition: вҲӮAОё Мё= вҲӮЛҷОё вҲӮП„ (13) Specifically, the empirical slip law V (П„) has no dependence on AОё (the left side vanishes), whereas ЛҷОё depends on П„ through V (the right side is non-zero). Therefore, RSF constitutes a non-associated dissipative system requiring two distinct dual dissipation potentials [21, 47]: 1.Slip Potential ОҰвҲ— V (П„; Оё): Governs the mechanical slip rate, with Оё as a parameter. 2.Healing Potential ОҰвҲ— Оё(AОё; П„, Оё): Governs the state evolution kinetics, with the current stress state (or equivalently V ) appearing as a parametric variable. The kinetic equations are given by the decoupled normality rules: V вүЎЛҷОҙp = вҲӮОҰвҲ— вҲӮП„ (14) ЛҷОё = вҲӮОҰвҲ— Оё вҲӮAОё (15) We define the state-dependent reference yield surface (threshold stress): П„th(Оё) = Пғn  f0 + b ln  Оё Оё0  (16) where Пғn is the effective normal stress (positive in compression), f0 is the reference friction coefficient, and a, b are the direct and evolution effect parameters, respectively. Derivation of the Dieterich Aging Law To recover the Aging Law exactly, we define the following bipotential pair: Slip Potential: V (П„; Оё) = aПғnV0 exp  П„ вҲ’П„th(Оё) aПғn  (17) Healing Potential: Оё(AОё; П„, Оё) = AОё  1 вҲ’ОёV0 Dc exp  П„ вҲ’П„th(Оё) aПғn  = AОё  1 вҲ’V Оё Dc  (18) where V in Eq. 18 is understood as the instantaneous slip velocity determined by the current П„ via Eq. 14. Slip Velocity (V ) Applying Eq. 14 to Eq. 17: вҲӮП„ = V0 exp  П„ вҲ’П„th(Оё) aПғn  (19) Inverting this yields the exact RSF stress equation: П„ = Пғn  f0 + a ln  + b ln  Оё Оё0  (20) Note that no asymptotic approximation is

text: Velocity (V ) Applying Eq. 14 to Eq. 17: вҲӮП„ = V0 exp  П„ вҲ’П„th(Оё) aПғn  (19) Inverting this yields the exact RSF stress equation: П„ = Пғn  f0 + a ln  + b ln  Оё Оё0  (20) Note that no asymptotic approximation is required here since the mechanical response is exactly decoupled from the state affinity AОё by construction. State Evolution Applying Eq. 15 to Eq. 18: ЛҷОё = вҲӮОҰвҲ— Оё вҲӮAОё = 1 вҲ’V Оё Dc (21) This is the exact Dieterich Aging Law. The non-associated structure is evident in the equations since the state evolution depends on the mechanical state (V ), but is derived from a potential distinct from that governing the slip itself. Derivation of the Ruina Slip Law The Slip Law requires zero healing when V = 0. We retain the same slip potential ОҰвҲ— V (Eq. 17) but modify the healing potential: Healing Potential (Slip Law): Оё(AОё; П„, Оё) = вҲ’AОё  V Оё Dc ln  V Оё Dc  (22) where V is again the instantaneous velocity from Eq. 19. State Evolution Differentiating Eq. 22: ЛҷОё = вҲӮОҰвҲ— Оё вҲӮAОё = вҲ’V Оё Dc ln  V Оё Dc  (23) This is the exact Ruina Slip Law. When V вҶ’0, the term V ln(V ) вҶ’0, enforcing ЛҷОё = 0 as required. Thermodynamic Admissibility and High-Velocity Breakdown The foundational requirement of any physically sound constitutive model is adherence to the Second Law of Thermodynamics. In the context of isothermal Generalized Solid Mechanics, the intrinsic dissipation D must satisfy the Clausius-Duhem inequality for all physically admissible states: D = П„V + AОё ЛҷОё вүҘ0 (24) Thermodynamic Breakdown of the Slip Law Evaluating the Ruina Slip Law exposes a critical theoretical limitation. Substituting the exact state evolution (Eq. 23) into the dissipation inequality yields: DSlip = П„V вҲ’bПғnDc Оё  V Оё Dc ln  V Оё Dc   П„ вҲ’bПғn ln  V Оё Dc  (25) We explicitly expand the logarithmic term and substitute the exact macroscopic stress П„ (Eq. 20): DSlip = V Пғn   f0 + a ln  + b ln  Оё Оё0  вҲ’b ln(V ) вҲ’b ln(Оё) + b ln(Dc)  (26) The state variable Оё cancels identically from the dissipation equation (+b ln(Оё) вҲ’b ln(Оё) = 0). Expanding the reference velocity and grouping the remaining

title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
text: V Пғn   f0 + a ln  + b ln  Оё Оё0  вҲ’b ln(V ) вҲ’b ln(Оё) + b ln(Dc)  (26) The state variable Оё cancels identically from the dissipation equation (+b ln(Оё) вҲ’b ln(Оё) = 0). Expanding the reference velocity and grouping the remaining terms results in an exact, purely velocity-dependent expression for the intrinsic dissipation: DSlip = V Пғn h f0 + (a вҲ’b) ln(V ) вҲ’a ln(V0) вҲ’b ln(Оё0) + b ln(Dc) i (27) This exact thermodynamic reduction reveals a fundamental physical violation at extreme slip rates. In the seismogenic regime, faults are characteristically velocity-weakening, necessitating that b > a. Consequently, the coefficient (a вҲ’b) is strictly negative. As the slip velocity approaches high velocity speeds (V вҶ’ вҲһ), the term (a вҲ’b) ln(V ) вҶ’вҲ’вҲһ. Since all other terms within the bracket are bounded constants, the total intrinsic dissipation inevitably becomes negative at sufficiently high velocities (though this may not be physically relevant for real faults) : lim V вҶ’вҲһDSlip вҶ’вҲ’вҲһ for b > a (28) This proves that the unregularized empirical Ruina Slip Law is not globally thermodynamically admissible. While the bipotential GSM framework flawlessly maps the algebraic structure of the empirical equations, it formally dictates that the logarithmic RSF formulation cannot govern dynamic rupture. To prevent violations of the Second Law of Thermodynamics, computational models must regularize the slip potential at high velocities (e.g., via sinh-based reaction rate theory) to ensure bounded, positive dissipation. TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks Admissibility of the Aging Law For the Dieterich Aging Law, substituting the state affinity (Eq. 12) and the exact state evolution (Eq. 21) yields: DAging = П„V + bПғnDc Оё  1 вҲ’V Оё Dc  = V (П„ вҲ’bПғn) + bПғnDc Оё (29) Substituting the macroscopic stress П„ (Eq. 20) and grouping terms gives: DAging = V Пғn  f0 вҲ’b + a ln  + b ln  Оё Оё0  + bПғnDc Оё (30) The term bПғnDc/Оё represents the rate of free energy dissipation associated with autonomous contact aging and is strictly positive for Оё > 0. During slip-weakening ( ЛҷОё < 0), the stored surface energy of the contacts is released, contributing negatively to the local dissipation budget, but the positive mechanical work (П„V ) globally dominates. The Aging Law thus remains thermodynamically admissible across standard operational regimes, though

title: TIP-INN: A Thermodynamics-informed Machine Learning Framework for the Mechanical Behavior of Rocks
text: is strictly positive for Оё > 0. During slip-weakening ( ЛҷОё < 0), the stored surface energy of the contacts is released, contributing negatively to the local dissipation budget, but the positive mechanical work (П„V ) globally dominates. The Aging Law thus remains thermodynamically admissible across standard operational regimes, though similar to the Slip law above, the aging law has the identical high-V breakdown. Regularization for Global Thermodynamic Admissibility The previous section demonstrated that the classical logarithmic Slip Law inevitably violates the Clausius-Duhem inequality at dynamic rupture velocities (V вҶ’вҲһ). To rectify this, the bipotential framework must be regularized at both asymptotic limits. We apply absolute reaction-rate theory to resolve the singularity at V вҶ’0, and demonstrate that elastodynamic (radiation) damping is required to enforce thermodynamic consistency at V вҶ’вҲһ. Reaction-Rate Regularization (The sinh Law) The empirical logarithmic slip equation implies a unidirectional crossing of asperity energy barriers, resulting in an anti-physical singularity at V = 0. Following transition-state theory [30, 41] and work in numerical fault modeling [55, 77], the slip potential must account for both forward and backward microscopic jumps. We construct a regularized, globally convex slip potential using the hyperbolic cosine: V (П„; Оё) = 2aПғnV0  cosh  П„ вҲ’П„th(Оё) aПғn  вҲ’1  (31) The subtraction of unity ensures that ОҰвҲ— V (П„th, Оё) = 0 and ОҰвҲ— 0 for all admissible states. Applying the normality rule (Eq. 14) yields the regularized slip equation: вҲӮП„ = 2V0 sinh  П„ вҲ’П„th(Оё) aПғn  (32) Inverting this provides the exact, regularized macroscopic shear stress: П„ = П„th(Оё) + aПғn arcsinh  = Пғn  f0 + a arcsinh  + b ln  Оё Оё0  (33) This regularization resolves the zero-velocity singularity, as arcsinh(0) = 0, allowing the interface to arrest without generating infinite negative stress. The Persistence of the High-Velocity Violation While the sinh potential grounds the near-zero velocity regime physically, we must evaluate its high-velocity thermodynamic behavior. Substituting the regularized stress (Eq. 33) into the dissipation expression for the Slip Law yields: DReg = V Пғn  f0 + a arcsinh  + b ln  Оё Оё0  вҲ’b ln  V Оё Dc  (34) Once again, the state variable Оё cancels identically. Regrouping terms gives: DReg = V Пғn  f0 + a arcsinh  вҲ’b ln(V ) вҲ’b ln(Оё0) + b ln(Dc)  (35) To evaluate the dynamic rupture

text: b ln  Оё Оё0  вҲ’b ln  V Оё Dc  (34) Once again, the state variable Оё cancels identically. Regrouping terms gives: DReg = V Пғn  f0 + a arcsinh  вҲ’b ln(V ) вҲ’b ln(Оё0) + b ln(Dc)  (35) To evaluate the dynamic rupture limit, we employ the asymptotic expansion of the inverse hyperbolic sine as V вҶ’вҲһ: arcsinh  = ln пЈ« + s V 2 + 1 пЈ¶ пЈё вҲ’вҲ’вҲ’вҲ’вҶ’ln  (36) Substituting this limit into the dissipation expression yields: lim V вҶ’вҲһDReg вүҲV Пғn [(a вҲ’b) ln(V ) вҲ’a ln(V0) вҲ’b ln(Оё0) + b ln(Dc)] (37) This is a critical theoretical result: the sinh regularization fails to prevent the Second Law violation at high velocities. Because the physical transition-state barrier is entirely overcome at extreme shear, the sinh function reverts to logarithmic scaling. In the velocity-weakening regime (b > a), the (a вҲ’b) ln(V ) term still drives the total intrinsic dissipation to negative infinity: lim V вҶ’вҲһDReg вҶ’вҲ’вҲһ for b > a (38) Thermodynamic Resolution via Viscous Damping The mathematical failure of both logarithmic and sinh potentials at high slip rates implies that frictional constitutive laws alone cannot describe dynamic rupture. parallel dissipative mechanism is required to bound the energy budget. Physically, at seismic slip rates (вҲј m/s), the fault interface radiates elastodynamic energy (radiation damping) and experiences phonon drag [76]. In the GSM framework, this is incorporated by adding a linear viscous stress О·V to the macroscopic stress, where О· вүҲG/2cs (shear modulus G, shear wave speed cs). The fully regularized macroscopic stress becomes: П„total = Пғn  f0 + a arcsinh  + b ln  Оё Оё0  | {z } П„RSF +О·V (39) The corresponding total intrinsic dissipation incorporates the viscous penalty: DT otal = П„RSF V + AОё ЛҷОё | {z } DReg +О·V 2 (40) At the dynamic rupture limit (V вҶ’вҲһ), the dominant terms are: DT otal вүҲО·V 2 вҲ’Пғn|a вҲ’b|V ln(V ) + O(V ) (41) Because the polynomial viscous dissipation (О·V 2) grows strictly faster than the logarithmic deficit (V ln V ), the quadratic term unconditionally dominates. Therefore: lim V вҶ’вҲһDT otal вҶ’+вҲһвүҘ0 (42) This proves that while the sinh potential ensures physical admissibility at V вҶ’0, the inclusion of high-velocity viscous (radiation) damping is strictly required to ensure global thermodynamic admissibility of the Rate-and-State framework across all slip regimes.

title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
text: Article https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-026-70033-x Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava п¬Ӯow interaction at Axial volcano Han Wu 1,2, Wenxin Xie1, Satish C. Singh , HГ©lГЁne Carton 1, Graham M. Kent1,3, Adrien F. Arnulf 4 & Alistair J. Harding5 Magmatically-accreted upper crust along mid-ocean ridges is traditionally considered to consist of lava п¬Ӯows overlying a sheeted-dyke complex. However, how the upper crust is formed at hotspot-inп¬Ӯuenced ridge segments remains unknown. Using three-dimensional seismic reп¬Ӯection data, here we report images of lava п¬Ӯow layering beneath Axial volcano on the Juan de Fuca Ridge. These lava п¬Ӯow layers dip inward towards the caldera and rift zones, resulting from subsidence caused by magma withdrawal beneath the summit and rift zone extension. Furthermore, our images reveal that the classic, laterally continuous sheeted-dyke complex is absent. Instead, lava п¬Ӯow layers are rotated and brought into direct contact with the magma domain, leading to partial assimilation of these units. Three-dimensional images also show that melt is injected outward along lava п¬Ӯow layers, suggesting that the upper crust at Axial volcano is formed by complex interactions between injected melt sills and lava п¬Ӯow layers. The oceanic crust, which comprises two-thirds of the EarthвҖҷs surface, is formed through partial melting of the mantle, where upwelling melt migrates towards the surface and accumulates in crustal magma reservoirs1вҖ“3. Some of this melt is erupted through feeder dykes, with erupted lava п¬Ӯows at the surface and intruded dykes forming the upper oceanic crust (Layer 2), while the residual melt cools through hydrothermal circulation and crystallises, forming the lower gabbroic crust (Layer 3)4. This architecture is the classic layer-cake oceanic crustal structure as described in the Penrose model5. Using ophiolite6 and drilling studies as a framework7,8, seismic investigations of the upper crust9вҖ“12 have further divided it into Layer 2A and Layer 2B, where Layer 2A is interpreted to consist of lava п¬Ӯows and Layer 2B of sheeted dykes, although hydrothermal alteration has also been invoked as a possible mechanism explaining the location of the seismically-deп¬Ғned Layer 2A/2B boundary13. Although lava п¬Ӯows and sheeted dyke sequences can be unambiguously distinguished within ophiolites, drill cores, and tectonic windows14,15, their in-situ characterisations using seismic methods have been difп¬Ғcult. This difп¬Ғculty is because lava п¬Ӯow stratigraphy has not been seismically imaged thus far, and vertical or steeply dipping dykes are inherently difп¬Ғcult to image using either reп¬Ӯected or refracted waves. Furthermore, the boundary between lava п¬Ӯow

title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
text: cores, and tectonic windows14,15, their in-situ characterisations using seismic methods have been difп¬Ғcult. This difп¬Ғculty is because lava п¬Ӯow stratigraphy has not been seismically imaged thus far, and vertical or steeply dipping dykes are inherently difп¬Ғcult to image using either reп¬Ӯected or refracted waves. Furthermore, the boundary between lava п¬Ӯow and dyke sequences is not sharp, instead there is a high velocity gradient zone separating low-velocity lava п¬Ӯow layers above with the high-velocity dyke sequence below4. Therefore, the main methods to locate the Layer 2A/2B boundary has been the stacking of wide-angle energy in multi-channel seismic (MCS) data produced by the high velocity gradient at Layer 2A/B boundary11 (Fig. 1a) or its identiп¬Ғcation is based on velocity contours and velocity gradients obtained using tomographic methods16 (Fig. 1b). In order to image lava п¬Ӯow stratigraphy, threedimensional (3D) pre-stack depth migration (PSDM) methodology is required to adequately refocus the seismic energy in the shallow crust (e.g., Fig. 1c), which we have carried out using 3D MCS data from Axial volcano17. Axial volcano lies at the intersection of the intermediatespreading Juan de Fuca Ridge and Cobb hotspot (Fig. 2) and seems to have been formed in the last ~0.5 Ma18вҖ“20. It hosts several hydrothermal п¬Ғelds21,22 and has been the site of three documented eruptions Received: 9 July 2025 Accepted: 13 February 2026 Check for updates 1UniversitГ© Paris CitГ©, Institut de Physique du Globe de Paris, CNRS, Paris, France. 2School of Earth Sciences and Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University, Zhuhai, China. 3Nevada Seismological Laboratory, MS-0174, University of Nevada, Reno, NV, USA. 4Amazon, San Diego, CA, USA. 5Cecil H. and Ida M. Green Institute of Geophysics and Planetary Physics, Scripps Institution of Oceanography, University of California San Diego, La Jolla, CA, USA. e-mail: singh@ipgp.fr Nature Communications| (2026) 17:3512 1234567890():,; 1234567890():,; (1998, 2011, 2015)23вҖ“26 with the next one considered imminent (as of late 2025) on the basis of geodetic-derived deformation and seismicity rates27. Axial volcano lies at a water depth of about 1.4 km; it displays a nearly п¬Ӯat, 20вҖ“40 km wide summit hosting a horseshoe-shaped 8 km Г— 3 km caldera that separates two rift zones: the north rift zone (NRZ) and south rift zone (SRZ) (Fig. 2)18,28. The current caldera is interpreted to have been formed 1259 +/вҲ’119 years BP, based on the ages of large volume lava п¬Ӯows on the distal SRZ29. The volcano is continuously monitored using a sea-to-land, п¬Ғber-optic cabled observatory, recording

title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
text: the north rift zone (NRZ) and south rift zone (SRZ) (Fig. 2)18,28. The current caldera is interpreted to have been formed 1259 +/вҲ’119 years BP, based on the ages of large volume lava п¬Ӯows on the distal SRZ29. The volcano is continuously monitored using a sea-to-land, п¬Ғber-optic cabled observatory, recording earthquake, geodetic, heat п¬Ӯow and chemical data30. In 1998, sparse wide-angle ocean-bottom seismic refraction data were acquired that showed the presence of a large magma body embedded within an up to 11-km-thick oceanic crust31. A 2D MCS dataset collected shortly thereafter in 2002 revealed complex magma sill reп¬Ӯections and a Layer 2A/2B boundary16,32,33, similar to images obtained along non-hotspot-inп¬Ӯuenced mid-ocean ridges (e.g., Fig. 1a, b)9вҖ“12. Based on an updated tomographic velocity model that combined prior on-bottom wide-angle31 and redatumed, 6-km-long 2D streamer data16, the Layer 2 A thickness was found to vary between 0.4 km and 1.4 km, with the thinnest Layer 2 A (0.4вҖ“0.7 km) observed just outside of the caldera with a slightly thicker Layer 2A (0.75вҖ“0.95 km) centered beneath the caldera32. However, as tomographic images only provide low-resolution information, it is not possible to decipher the п¬Ғne-scale lava п¬Ӯow layering of this uppermost oceanic crust and investigate relationships to the underlying magmatic system. In 2019, an exceptional 3D MCS dataset was acquired at Axial volcano (Fig. 2; Supplementary Fig. 1), showing the presence of four funnel-shaped melt boundaries beneath the volcano17, interpreted as the top of a magma domain17, within whichmelt sills are distributed in a matrix of possibly crystal mush, and representing the crustal lithosphere-asthenosphere boundary (LAB). Here, we processed the same MCS dataset differently using 3D Kirchhoff PSDM (see Methods), instead of post-stack time migration17. Our processed 3D seismic volume provides the clear images of lava п¬Ӯow stratigraphy within the upper crust and its relationship to the complex underlying magma domain, suggesting that the upper oceanic crust is formed by a complex interaction between melt sills and the extrusive layer within a ridge-hotspot environment. Results and discussion Lava п¬Ӯow layering The 3D PSDM reveals lava п¬Ӯow layering between the seaп¬Ӯoor and the magma domain (Supplementary Movies 1 and 2). A block diagram (Fig. 3, Supplementary Fig. 2 and Movie 3) shows lava п¬Ӯow layering beneath the northern (Inline 341) and southern (Inline 178) rift zones, and along a perpendicular proп¬Ғle (Xline 2601) through the caldera. We identify the lava п¬Ӯow layering based on its reп¬Ӯection

title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
text: Movies 1 and 2). A block diagram (Fig. 3, Supplementary Fig. 2 and Movie 3) shows lava п¬Ӯow layering beneath the northern (Inline 341) and southern (Inline 178) rift zones, and along a perpendicular proп¬Ғle (Xline 2601) through the caldera. We identify the lava п¬Ӯow layering based on its reп¬Ӯection characteristics and seismic attributes (Supplementary Fig. 3, see Methods). The top of the highly reп¬Ӯective zone, interpreted as a volume containing melt sills, deп¬Ғnes the top of the magma domain, representing the lithosphere-asthenosphere boundary (LAB) (Figs. 2вҖ“4)17. Although the term LAB is generally used elsewhere to deп¬Ғne the base of the lithosphere in the upper mantle, here a crustal melt sill beneath the ridge axis separates solid brittle crust above with partially molten lower crust and upper mantle below, and therefore the top of the magma domain at Axial volcano is termed as the LAB17. North of the caldera, lava п¬Ӯow layers from the southeast dip toward the NRZ before becoming п¬Ӯat beneath the NRZ (Fig. 3). Similarly, south of the caldera, lava п¬Ӯow layers dip gently inwards toward the rift zone, indicating a symmetric pattern. The perpendicular Xline proп¬Ғle shows that the layers continue beneath the caldera, but in some cases are offset by small-throw steeply dipping faults with vertical offsets of tens of metres. Four Xlines spaced 5 km apart across the caldera exhibit a similar pattern of lava п¬Ӯow layering in the upper crust, with a pervasive inward-dipping geometry towards the magma domain beneath the caldera (Fig. 4, Supplementary Fig. 4 and Movie 4). As the dip of the layers increases with depth (Fig. 5), some layers have deepened up to 1 km through rotation, with the overall stack of lava п¬Ӯow layers reaching >3 km in thickness. The pattern of inward-dipping lava п¬Ӯow layers towards the magma domain extends southeast of the caldera as seen along Xline proп¬Ғle 2424 (Fig. 4). The dip of these layers varies, depending upon the location within the 3D box, ranging from nearly п¬Ӯat just below the seaп¬Ӯoor to dips of up to 18В° near the magma domain (Fig. 5). Sometimes, these layers п¬Ӯatten as they approach the magma domain and beneath the rift zones. Rift zone-coincident proп¬Ғles were extracted from the 3D PSDM volume (Fig. 6, Supplementary Fig. 5), which highlight the relationship between lava п¬Ӯow layering, faults, and the underlying magma domain. Although the images along the NRZ are

title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
text: these layers п¬Ӯatten as they approach the magma domain and beneath the rift zones. Rift zone-coincident proп¬Ғles were extracted from the 3D PSDM volume (Fig. 6, Supplementary Fig. 5), which highlight the relationship between lava п¬Ӯow layering, faults, and the underlying magma domain. Although the images along the NRZ are relatively poor, layered structures are present. In contrast, layering is very prominent along the SRZ proп¬Ғle down to 3 km below the seaп¬Ӯoor (bsf). The analysis of the 3D PSDM volume indicates that the upper crustal layered stratigraphy is present over more than 85% of the study area; only the northeast corner of the 3D box does not exhibit strong layering. Given the frequency bandwidth of our seismic images (5вҖ“30 Hz), individual lava п¬Ӯows cannot be imaged, but rather the visible layering likely represents packages of п¬Ӯows, with vertical resolution dependent on wavelength. The vertical resolution is generally a quarter of the wavelength, which for an upper crustal velocity of 3вҖ“5 km/s would be ~25вҖ“250 m (Supplementary Fig. 3). Wideaperture (6 km source-receiver offset) 3D imaging allows us to image structure on similar lateral scales (250 m). Magma domain and melt sill injection Using images from the 3D post-stack time-migrated volume, Kent et al.17 observed four large-scale funnel-shaped boundaries that deп¬Ғne the top of a magma domain. The 3D PSDM imagery presented here conп¬Ғrms those features but also reveals other smaller melt sills of various sizes outside of the identiп¬Ғed magma domain17, which suggests that the inп¬Ӯuence of the magma domain may extend further east of the caldera (Fig. 7, Supplementary Fig. 6 and Movie 5). For example, within proп¬Ғle Inline 373 shown in the bottom left of Fig. 7, there are six sill-like melt bodies imaged; but only two of the most southeasterly melt sills were previously reported17. A critical observation is that the lava п¬Ӯow layers not only abut the sides of the magma domain but, in some locations, melt appears to be injected outwards between lava п¬Ӯow layers above the magma domain (Fig. 8, Supplementary Fig. 7). However, it is sometimes difп¬Ғcult to distinguish between the lava п¬Ӯow layers, injected melt sills between lava п¬Ӯow layers, and small melt sills that form the top of the magma domain. Lava п¬Ӯow layering within the caldera above the magma domain is more chaotic than outside the caldera, apparently disrupted by magmatic and hydrothermal processes (dyking, sill injection) or by faulting and block rotation during caldera collapse events (Fig. 8, Inline 241). title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
text: is more chaotic than outside the caldera, apparently disrupted by magmatic and hydrothermal processes (dyking, sill injection) or by faulting and block rotation during caldera collapse events (Fig. 8, Inline 241). Lava п¬Ӯow layering, faults and rotated layers are clearly visible on seismic images (Fig. 7, Inline 241). Some of the shallow faults above the magma domain are distinctly imaged, with curved geometries and rotated layers (Fig. 8, inset). Away from the caldera, one can clearly see interaction between lava п¬Ӯow layers and melt sill intrusions (Fig. 7, Inline 373; Fig. 8, Inline 424). On the other hand, vertical dykes and hydrothermal conduits are inherently difп¬Ғcult to image. Subsidence Mapping of lava п¬Ӯows from the past three eruptions23вҖ“26 shows that these events initiated from dyke intrusion along the eastern edge of the caldera34, possibly sourced from the shallowest part of the magma domain17. From that nucleation point, the dykes propagated either north or south along the rift zones for distances up to 20вҖ“30 km from the caldera with lava outpouring from п¬Ғssures along the rifts24вҖ“26. Within the caldera, recent sheet п¬Ӯows have thicknesses of 3вҖ“5 m near eruptive features increasing to ~15 m down the slope25,26,35,36. Awayfrom the caldera, lava п¬Ӯows erupted along the rift zones form pillow mounds and ridges with thickness reaching >120 m24,26. Even though the seaп¬Ӯoor smoothly dips away from the caldera, the seismically imaged lava п¬Ӯow layers show a gentle dip either towards the magma domain, rift zones, and/or the caldera. The lava п¬Ӯow piles thicken as they approach the rift zones and the underlying magma domain, with the largest subsidence evident along the rift zones and within the caldera. This geometry suggests that the lava п¬Ӯow layers have subsided after their formation. There are two likely mechanisms that can cause the observed localised subsidence: (1) extension along rift zones, and (2) emptying of magma reservoirs during occasional large-volume eruptions that cause caldera collapse. As the lava п¬Ӯow layering in the seismic imagery generally thickens toward the caldera and rift zones, it is likely that this pattern is simply the result of inп¬Ғlling of space caused by the subsidence. However, the terminations of the deepest imaged lava п¬Ӯow layer are not always located beneath the rift zones; instead, some are shifted either towards the caldera or the large melt funnels17. The horizontal wavelength of subsidence is on the scale of 10вҖ“15 km, and such long-wavelength

title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
text: caused by the subsidence. However, the terminations of the deepest imaged lava п¬Ӯow layer are not always located beneath the rift zones; instead, some are shifted either towards the caldera or the large melt funnels17. The horizontal wavelength of subsidence is on the scale of 10вҖ“15 km, and such long-wavelength subsidence is likely due to the cumulative effects of major withdrawal events from the sub-caldera magma domain (Fig. 9). Ridge-ward dipping lava п¬Ӯow layers have also been observed in Iceland, both east and west of the ridge axis37,38. There too, the dip of lava п¬Ӯow layers increases with depth from nearly horizontal at shallow depth to 5вҖ“10В° over 10вҖ“15 km distance. Drilling results indicate that lava п¬Ӯow layers that lie at 1.5вҖ“2.0 km depth were deposited on land or in shallow water environment39,40. Gradual sagging of lava п¬Ӯow layers due to continuous loading of lava п¬Ӯows in the rift zone was invoked to explain deepening of lava п¬Ӯows towards the rift zone for Iceland37,41, but here we show that emptying of magma reservoir during large volcanic eruption might be the main process for the increasing dips towards the rift zones and caldera. Seaward dipping reп¬Ӯections (SDRs) commonly observed along volcanic continental margins42,43 resemble the dipping lava п¬Ӯow reп¬Ӯections observed at Axial volcano. Taken together, the lava п¬Ӯow layering in Iceland and at Axial volcano may represent a special class of crustal accretion at ocean spreading centres occurring along magmatically enhanced sections of mid-ocean ridges, such as those inп¬Ӯuenced by ridge-plume interaction41,44,45. Assimilation On the basis of seismic reп¬Ӯection images of the funnel-shaped upper surface of the magma domain observed down to 6 km depth, Kent et al.17 hypothesised that assimilation of dykes and gabbroic rocks has been ongoing, as a consequence of a phase of enhanced magma supply currently taking place at Axial volcano. If these lava п¬Ӯow layers were initially emplaced at the seaп¬Ӯoor, they would have had higher porosities46,47 п¬Ғlled with water39,40. As they get buried, heated, and altered40, they eventually lie in contact with the top of the magma domain. The melting temperature of hydrated lava п¬Ӯows is ~800 В°C, much less than that of primitive basalt (>1100 В°C)48, therefore the contact zone between hydrated lava п¬Ӯow layers and melt within the magma domain could constitute a zone of interaction with assimilation of the lava п¬Ӯow layers directly into the magma domain48 (Fig. 9). Although some primitive lavas erupted during the 2015 eruption, most of the lava emplaced during the three historical eruptions is somewhat chemically evolved36. At this time, there is no direct geochemical evidence of assimilation in the basalt chemistry of erupted lava п¬Ӯows at Axial volcano, the presence of a young andesite ridge along the NRZ north of Axial volcano49 suggests potential remelting and assimilation and/or a high degree of fractionation49, possibly supporting the assimilation hypothesis. Elsewhere, the assimilation of some of the sheeted dyke sequence into the underlying magma chamber has been documented from ophiolites and laboratory experiments48,50, but the possible assimilation of basaltic lava п¬Ӯows has not been considered until now. Iceland is an archetypical example for the observation of remelting and assimilation of crustal rocks, with at least 10% of lavas of rhyolitic origin51,52. Rhyolitic melt was unexpectedly encountered at 2.1 km

title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
text: ophiolites and laboratory experiments48,50, but the possible assimilation of basaltic lava п¬Ӯows has not been considered until now. Iceland is an archetypical example for the observation of remelting and assimilation of crustal rocks, with at least 10% of lavas of rhyolitic origin51,52. Rhyolitic melt was unexpectedly encountered at 2.1 km depth during the Kraп¬Ӯa Testbed Drilling project in 200953,54. Although several hypotheses have been proposed for the origin of these rhyolites, melting and assimilation of wall rock51 or partial melting of hydrated and hydrothermally altered crust53 remain the main hypotheses. Assimilation has also been reported in the Oman ophiolite4,48, at a slow spreading ridge55 and at an ultra-slow spreading ridge56 and hence assimilation might be more widespread along the mid-ocean ridge system than previously realised, especially at sites with enhanced magma supply, such as those located at a ridge-plume intersections. Hydrated-dehydrated boundary So where is the sheeted-dyke complex? Although some vertical dykes must be present above the magma domain and along the rift zones, the imaging of thick lava п¬Ӯow layers, some of which are in direct contact with the magma domain, suggests that the classic thick sheeted dyke sequence of Layer 2B is absent beneath Axial volcano, and the crust predominantly consists of lava п¬Ӯows and gabbro. This observation is at odds with previous studies of the upper crust at Axial volcano16,32, where the upper crust was inferred to consist of two layers: lava п¬Ӯows and sheeted dyke complex. Instead, the 3D seismic imagery presented here suggests that the upper crust is mostly formed by lava п¬Ӯows and melt-sill interaction. It is certain that dykes feed magma to the surface during eruptions, but a combination of dyke assimilation and lateral sill intrusions within the lava п¬Ӯow layers may erase the evidence of the dyking process. However, since dykes are the main conduits of erupted lavas, extending for tens of kilometres along the rift zones, a dyke sequence might be present along the rift zones away from the summit of Axial volcano and along the non-hotspot-inп¬Ӯuenced ridge segments of the Juan du Fuca Ridge. Based on a comprehensive tomographic study16, it was suggested that the maximum thickness of Layer 2A (i.e., presumed lava п¬Ӯows) was ~1.4 km. However, 3D seismic reп¬Ӯection images show lava п¬Ӯow layering down to >3 km depth bsf, suggesting that the conventional Layer 2A/ 2B boundary does not represent a lava п¬ӮowвҖ“sheeted dyke boundary beneath Axial

title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
text: it was suggested that the maximum thickness of Layer 2A (i.e., presumed lava п¬Ӯows) was ~1.4 km. However, 3D seismic reп¬Ӯection images show lava п¬Ӯow layering down to >3 km depth bsf, suggesting that the conventional Layer 2A/ 2B boundary does not represent a lava п¬ӮowвҖ“sheeted dyke boundary beneath Axial volcano. Instead, it may correspond to the depth of melt sill injection into the lava п¬Ӯows. We envision that when hot molten rock (melt sills) is injected into the upper crustal lava п¬Ӯow layers, the heat would metamorphose and dehydrate the nearby lava п¬Ӯow layers. This would make the rock denser and increase the contrast in seismic velocities. Over time, as more melt sills are injected and solidify, the seismic velocity differences between the altered lava п¬Ӯow layers and the solidiп¬Ғed melt sills would gradually diminish. The combined effect would progressively increase the seismic velocities of deeper layers, which could explain the velocity boundary that creates the classical Layer 2A/2B seismic reп¬Ӯections observed at Axial volcano (Figs. 1b and 10). Such intrusion of melt sills into lava п¬Ӯow layers has also been observed in the lower sequences of Icelandic lava п¬Ӯows57, and in SDRs along volcanic continental margins58. Subsequent metamorphism (in the greenschist and amphibolite facies) leads to a signiп¬Ғcant increase in the P-wave velocity at this contact surface58. To further investigate the transition between shallow lava п¬Ӯows and their deeper intruded counterparts, seismic full waveform inversion (FWI) was performed along one of the long-offset 2D proп¬Ғles (having a maximum offset of 12 km, see Methods), to determine the high-resolution P-wave velocity structure of the upper crust (Fig. 10). By combining conventional imaging and FWI results, we п¬Ғnd that the conventional base of Layer 2A from stacking of wide-angle reп¬Ӯection data corresponds to a high Fig. 9 | A model of crustal accretion at plume-inп¬Ӯuenced ridges. Article https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-026-70033-x Nature Communications| (2026) 17:3512 velocity gradient zone, where the P-wave velocity increases from 4.1 km/s to 6.0 km/s at a depth of ~1 km below the seaп¬Ӯoor, with a decrease in porosity from 18% to 4% (Supplementary Fig. 8)46,47. This rapid vertical change in P-wave velocity within the lava п¬Ӯow stratigraphy supports the hypothesis that the Layer 2A/2B boundary beneath Axial volcano may be a dehydration/metamorphic boundary. Our п¬Ғndings support an interpretation that the shallow crust at Axial volcano differs from the classic model of magmatic crustal accretion at mid-ocean ridges. We interpret that the upper crust at Axial volcano does not consist of the expected lava п¬Ӯows overlying a sheeted dyke complex, but instead, its formation is primarily governed by the interaction between lava п¬Ӯows and melt sills injected into the lava п¬Ӯow layers and by subsequent metamorphism. Thus, these insights into the active shallow processes at Axial volcano provide a template for understanding the evolution of ridge segments or rift zones dominated by a central volcano, including hotspot-inп¬Ӯuenced ones, as well as large igneous provinces such as Iceland. Our shallow crustal observations complement the deep crustal LAB imaging of Kent et al.17 from the same dataset. Together, these studies suggest that Axial volcano represents an endmember of crustal accretion during strong magmatic phases, where assimilation along a thermally-controlled LAB17 and melt sill injection into lava п¬Ӯow layers, reported in this study, dominate over classical dyke-dominated accretion (Fig. 9). As magmatism wanes, this system may revert to a more classical crustal architecture. Methods Data acquisition In 2019, 3D multi-channel seismic (MCS) data were acquired during the Axial-3D Expedition (NSF-funded cruise MGL1905) aboard the R/V Marcus Langseth. The acquisition was designed to provide highresolution subsurface imaging of the Axial volcano region, using a state-of-the-art seismic array with careful planning to ensure high data quality and complete 3D coverage. Acquisition geometry. The seismic acquisition system consisted of four 5.85-km-long hydrophone streamers spaced at 150 m. These streamers were equipped with a total of 468 channels per streamer, spaced at 12.5 m intervals. The conп¬Ғguration allowed for dense spatial sampling, minimising spatial aliasing

title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
text: high data quality and complete 3D coverage. Acquisition geometry. The seismic acquisition system consisted of four 5.85-km-long hydrophone streamers spaced at 150 m. These streamers were equipped with a total of 468 channels per streamer, spaced at 12.5 m intervals. The conп¬Ғguration allowed for dense spatial sampling, minimising spatial aliasing and providing comprehensive data coverage across the survey area (Supplementary Fig. 9). The source array featured two п¬Ӯip-п¬Ӯopping airgun arrays, each with a total volume of 54.1 L. These arrays were towed at a spacing of 75 m across the shipвҖҷs centreline, providing an alternating source conп¬Ғguration that increased acquisition efп¬Ғciency (Supplementary Fig. 9). The depths of the source and receivers were carefully chosen to optimise signal п¬Ғdelity and minimise noise. The source depth of 12 m was selected to enhance the low-frequency energy output of the airguns, crucial for imaging deeper geological structures. The streamer depth of 16 m provided a balance between signal strength at lower frequencies and suppression of surface-generated noise. Survey area and coverage. The survey covered a 40 km by 16.3 km area (Fig. 2, Supplementary Fig. 1) constructed from53primary sail-line passes to ensure high-quality baseline coverage. To minimise data gaps caused by navigation or acquisition challenges, 10 additional Inline passes were performed, strategically placed to improve coverage uniformity. Furthermore, 6 lines were reacquired to address areas with incomplete data, ensuring comprehensive and reliable coverage throughout the entire study area. These data were binned at 12.5 m Г— 37.5 m, providing 3200 Xlines (1001вҖ“4200) and 438 Inlines (101вҖ“538), within a framework where the Inlines were spaced at 37.5 m and Xlines at 12.5 m (Supplementary Fig. 9). The fold (numbers of traces in each bin) was around 78 (Supplementary Fig. 9d), which provided a good signal to noise ratio after processing. Data quality measures. To ensure high-quality data acquisition, realtime quality control was conducted during the cruise. This included monitoring the positions of streamers and sources, analysing navigation accuracy, and continuously evaluating the recorded data for noise or signal issues. The multi-pass design and reshoot strategy further ensured data continuity and completeness, reducing uncertainties in the п¬Ғnal seismic dataset. Initial velocity model construction An initial interval velocity model, derived from a prior high-resolution 3D tomographic inversion study16, was used for post-stack time migration imaging, and was the subject of a recent publication by Kent et al.17. This model accurately captured the large-scale subsurface velocity structure,

text: the п¬Ғnal seismic dataset. Initial velocity model construction An initial interval velocity model, derived from a prior high-resolution 3D tomographic inversion study16, was used for post-stack time migration imaging, and was the subject of a recent publication by Kent et al.17. This model accurately captured the large-scale subsurface velocity structure, providing a reliable baseline for further reп¬Ғnement. The model, centred on the southeast edge of the Axial volcano caldera (45.92В°N, 129.99В°W), covered a 30 km Г— 40 km area, rotated 12.9В°N, with a grid interval of 100 m (Supplementary Fig. 10a). This velocity model was downloaded from the Marine Geoscience Data System (MGDS)16. However, this velocity model contained regions where velocity values were set to NaN (Not a Number) due to insufп¬Ғcient ray coverage. To adapt this model for our study, a series of steps was undertaken to rectify this problem, improve the resolution, and adapt its applicability within the study area (Supplementary Fig. 10). A specialised interpolation algorithm59 was applied to replace missing data (NaN values) by extrapolating from neighbouring, non-missing data points. This ensured spatial continuity while maintaining the geophysical plausibility of the velocity п¬Ғeld (Supplementary Figs. 10a, b). Out-of-bounds velocity assignment. For regions within our study area, but outside the original velocity modelвҖҷs spatial coverage, an average one-dimensional (1-D) velocity proп¬Ғle beneath the seaп¬Ӯoor was used to populate the velocity п¬Ғeld. This approach provided reasonable velocities in the unmodelled areas (Supplementary Fig. 10b). 3D smoothing. 3D smoothing was employed to address abrupt velocity anomalies and reduce noise in the velocity model. This process smoothed irregular interfaces, while preserving key geological features, such as the melt lens and the Layer 2A/2B boundary (Supplementary Fig. 10b). Water layer. The seaп¬Ӯoor depth was constrained using high-resolution bathymetric data (https:// www.gebco.net). The velocity of the water column above the seaп¬Ӯoor was uniformly set to 1.5 km/s. High-resolution resampling. The velocity model was reп¬Ғned to match the resolution of the data acquisition geometry. Using 3D interpolation, the model was reconstructed with п¬Ғner grid intervals of 12.5 m (Xline), 37.5 m (Inline), and 5 m (depth) (Supplementary Fig. 10c). This higher resolution velocity model ensured its compatibility with the seismic acquisition parameters and subsequently helped in improving the accuracy of seismic imaging. Data pre-processing A systematic pre-processing workп¬Ӯow was employed to enhance signal quality and mitigate noise, addressing challenges speciп¬Ғc to marine seismic datasets. Key steps are outlined below: Data interpolation. Interpolation was performed to compensate for gaps caused by dead channels with zero seismic energy. A dipcoherency-based algorithm was utilised, ensuring that both п¬Ӯat and dipping events in shot gathers could be adequately interpolated. Supplementary Fig. 11 shows a gather before and after the data interpolation, showing that the dead traces have been replaced by live traces, with coherent energy. Swell noise attenuation. The raw recorded data have low-frequency swell noise, which was removed using an expectation-maximisation algorithm60, which statistically separates coherent signal from random noise. Once the noise is detected based on the calculated probability threshold, it is rescaled to match the mean value of the regular data, effectively reducing the impact of swell noise and enhancing the overall data quality. Supplementary Fig. 12 shows a gather before and after swell noise removal, showing cleaner signals. Predictive deconvolution. To remove the ringing bubble pulse generated by the airgun source, two predictive deconvolution п¬Ғlters were computed for each sail line вҖ“ one for the port source and one for the starboard source. These п¬Ғlters were calibrated using an autocorrelation analysis of the source wavelet to ensure optimal suppression of the ringing effect (3вҖ“75 Hz). Supplementary Fig. 13 shows an example of gather before and after the predictive deconvolution, showing a signiп¬Ғcant decrease in the low-frequency bubble pulse noise. Spherical divergence correction. Seismic energy decays away from the source due to geometrical spreading. To address the amplitude decay caused by geometric spreading, a spherical divergence correction was applied using RMS (root-mean-square) velocities derived from tomographic

title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
text: after the predictive deconvolution, showing a signiп¬Ғcant decrease in the low-frequency bubble pulse noise. Spherical divergence correction. Seismic energy decays away from the source due to geometrical spreading. To address the amplitude decay caused by geometric spreading, a spherical divergence correction was applied using RMS (root-mean-square) velocities derived from tomographic interval velocity, using the following formula: Acomp tГ° Гһ = ArawГ°tГһ  vrmsГ°tГһ  t v0  2 , where, Acomp and Araw represent the amplitudes before and after compensation, vrms is the root mean square (RMS) velocity, v0 is the reference velocity, which is 1.5 km/s. t is the two-travel travel time at zero offset. Linear dipping noise removal. As there is a strong velocity contrast at the seaп¬Ӯoor and the basaltic seaп¬Ӯoor is rough, seaп¬Ӯoor scattering and diffraction occur that travel in the water column with water velocity, leading to linear noise (e.g., Supplementary Fig. 12a). This linear dipping noise was removed using a dip п¬Ғlter in the shot domain. The method used isolated and suppressed unwanted energy along speciп¬Ғc slopes (<1.8 km/s) in the frequency-wavenumber (f-k) domain, preserving primary reп¬Ӯections. Supplementary Fig. 14 shows a gather before and after the linear dipping noise removal. Suppression of energy travelling with low velocities. Abnormal energy travelling with low velocities (1.5 km/s), such as side-scattering noise and mode-converted events, were identiп¬Ғed and excluded in the common midpoint (CMP) gather domain using a f-k (frequencywavenumber) п¬Ғltering technique. The CMP gathers were п¬Ғrst corrected for normal moveout using the RMS velocity derived from 80% of the tomographic velocity. Consequently, the abnormal low-velocity signals appeared as downward-dipping components, which were then removed using a dip п¬Ғlter, followed by an inverse normal moveout correction. Supplementary Fig. 15 shows a gather before and after the suppression of energy travelling with low velocities. Frequency optimisation. To improve signal clarity, a Butterworth bandpass п¬Ғlter with a frequency range of 3вҖ“35 Hz was applied. This п¬Ғlter was chosen for its smooth roll-off and minimal phase distortion, ensuring that primary seismic reп¬Ӯections remained intact within the optimised frequency band. Surgical muting. Refracted and surface-related multiple energy were suppressed through a surgical mute, interactively applied based on amplitude and velocity characteristics. This step ensured that unwanted signals were excluded without compromising the integrity of the target reп¬Ӯections. 3D pre-stack depth migration (PSDM) We applied a 3D Kirchhoff PSDM61, employing a detailed workп¬Ӯow to ensure accurate subsurface imaging and optimal use

title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
text: a surgical mute, interactively applied based on amplitude and velocity characteristics. This step ensured that unwanted signals were excluded without compromising the integrity of the target reп¬Ӯections. 3D pre-stack depth migration (PSDM) We applied a 3D Kirchhoff PSDM61, employing a detailed workп¬Ӯow to ensure accurate subsurface imaging and optimal use of the dataset. Key aspects of the methodology are outlined below: Migration aperture. To ensure comprehensive subsurface imaging, migration was performed with an Inline aperture of 6 km and a Xline aperture of 1 km. We systematically tested Xline apertures of 1 km, 2 km, and 4 km (Supplementary Fig. 16). Increasing the Xline aperture enhanced the sharpness of steeply dipping structures (i.e., lithosphereasthenosphere boundary) but also ampliп¬Ғed shallow migration artifacts (smiles) that disrupted imaging of the volcanic stratigraphy (Supplementary Fig. 16). However, a narrow aperture led to lateral smearing. To achieve an optimal balance between structural clarity and an objective interpretation, the 1-km aperture image was selected for our п¬Ғnal interpretation while using the 4-km aperture image to avoid interpreting smeared image. This choice ensures sufп¬Ғcient data redundancy for coherent imaging while maintaining computational efп¬Ғciency. Article https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-026-70033-x Nature Communications| (2026) 17:3512 Velocity model reп¬Ғnement. The velocity model was iteratively reп¬Ғned through residual move-out (RMO) analysis applied to common-image gathers (CIGs), obtained after the application of PSDM. If the migration velocity is correct, reп¬Ӯection events in the CIG domain would become п¬Ӯat (i.e., horizontal in depth). Therefore, misaligned reп¬Ӯection events were systematically corrected by adjusting the velocity model, ensuring accurate alignment of events across all offsets. The updated model improved the consistency and focus of migrated reп¬Ӯections. Supplementary Fig. 17 shows a CIG before and after this correction. Muting strategy. After migration, far offset arrivals were stretched in depth. An aggressive muting strategy was applied to the CIGs to enhance image quality. This process selectively retained near-offset data, minimising the impact of long-offset noise and artifacts. The mute was designed to make sure all the wide-angle energy associated with Layer 2A was excluded. The muting criteria were interactively determined based on velocity analysis and reп¬Ӯection continuity. Supplementary Fig. 18 shows an example of a surgical mute used on these data. Imaging. A 3D Kirchhoff PSDM61 was performed by tracing ray-paths through the reп¬Ғned velocity model to accurately position reп¬Ӯection events in their true subsurface locations. The migration process transforms the pre-stack seismic data into CIGs in the depth domain with

title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
text: an example of a surgical mute used on these data. Imaging. A 3D Kirchhoff PSDM61 was performed by tracing ray-paths through the reп¬Ғned velocity model to accurately position reп¬Ӯection events in their true subsurface locations. The migration process transforms the pre-stack seismic data into CIGs in the depth domain with high spatial resolution (37.5 m Г— 12.5 m Г— 5 m). These CIGs were summed to produce 3D PSDM image (Supplementary Movies 1 and 2). This method captured structural details, while preserving amplitude variations, critical for subsequent interpretation and analysis. Kirchhoff versus Reverse Time Migration (RTM). One advantage of the Kirchhoff migration method is its computational efп¬Ғciency. However, as it is based on ray theory, which does not allow it to accurately compute travel times and amplitudes in the case of high velocity contrasts, such as basaltic seaп¬Ӯoor and melt bodies, its application may lead to migration artefacts such as smearing and migration smiles. Supplementary Fig. 16 shows that the smearing is more prominent when the aperture in the crossline direction is small (1 km), whereas the migration smiles in the upper part of the image are more pronounced when the aperture is large (4 km). On the other hand, reverse time migration (RTM)62 is based on the two-way wave equation, which allows to accurately model wave propagation in the case of complex geology and high velocity contrasts, and hence RTM has the potential to produce a better quality image. However, it is computationally very expensive as it based on numerical modelling of the wave equation. To assess the validity of our approach, we performed 2D PSDM using both Kirchhoff and RTM methods. By analysing our 3D PSDM image, we found that the smearing and migration smiles are more prominent in the Xline direction, possibly due to the coarser spatial sampling in the crossline direction (37.5 m), and therefore, we decided to test the two methods on a 2D proп¬Ғle acquired along the 3D box crossline direction (proп¬Ғle 2003), which was acquired using similar acquisition parameters. The results are shown in Supplementary Fig. 19. We п¬Ғnd that the 2D Kirchhoff image (Supplementary Fig. 19a) shows weak layered reп¬Ӯections above the magma domain; these features are more pronounced in the 2D RTM image (Supplementary Fig. 19b), but to a comparable degree as in the 3D Kirchoff image (Supplementary Fig. 16a), which gives us conп¬Ғdence that we could interpret the 3D PSDM

text: image (Supplementary Fig. 19a) shows weak layered reп¬Ӯections above the magma domain; these features are more pronounced in the 2D RTM image (Supplementary Fig. 19b), but to a comparable degree as in the 3D Kirchoff image (Supplementary Fig. 16a), which gives us conп¬Ғdence that we could interpret the 3D PSDM image for structures above the magma domain. Criteria for the identiп¬Ғcation of different events in seismic proп¬Ғles To systematically identify key geological features in seismic proп¬Ғles, we took seismic traces extracted from Inline 261 as examples, focusing on Xline locations 2275, 2775, and 3350 (Supplementary Fig. 3). These proп¬Ғles were examined using multiple seismic attributes, including waveform characteristics, instantaneous amplitude, instantaneous phase, and time-wavenumber spectra. The classiп¬Ғcation of seismic events was based on signal continuity, and distinct amplitude and phase properties, allowing for the differentiation of seaп¬Ӯoor, lava п¬Ӯow layers, melt sills injected into lava п¬Ӯow layering and the axial magma lens (AML) | lithosphere-asthenosphere boundaries (LAB) reп¬Ӯections. Seaп¬Ӯoor identiп¬Ғcation. The seaп¬Ӯoor reп¬Ӯection is the most prominent feature in the seismic image, characterised by the strongest instantaneous amplitude response in the dataset; an instantaneous phase transition from negative to positive; and the highest wavenumber among identiп¬Ғed events (Supplementary Fig. 3). These characteristics ensure a clear and unambiguous identiп¬Ғcation of the seaп¬Ӯoor within the whole data volume. Lava п¬Ӯow layer identiп¬Ғcation. Lava п¬Ӯow layers exhibit distinct seismic attributes that set them apart from deeper geological structures: an intermediate amplitude, lower than the seaп¬Ӯoor and LAB, but still clearly discernible; a phase transition from negative to positive, similar to the seaп¬Ӯoor; relatively high wavenumber, indicating a sharper and more abrupt change in subsurface properties (Supplementary Fig. 3). Their lateral continuity suggests that they generally initiate near the seaп¬Ӯoor and gently dip towards the magma domain or rift zones, with the dip varying from nearly zero reaching up to 18В° (Fig. 5). These features are essential in distinguishing lava п¬Ӯow layers from deeper seismic events, particularly when considering their spatial distribution and continuity. LAB identiп¬Ғcation. The LAB is a crucial structural interface in our seismic image analysis, displaying as a moderately strong amplitude event, lower than the seaп¬Ӯoor but still well-deп¬Ғned; a phase transition from positive to negative, contrasting with the seaп¬Ӯoor and lava п¬Ӯow layers; a lower wavenumber compared to the overlying features, indicative of a broader-scale transition (Supplementary Fig. 3). These characteristics facilitate the identiп¬Ғcation of the LAB in the seismic

title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
text: event, lower than the seaп¬Ӯoor but still well-deп¬Ғned; a phase transition from positive to negative, contrasting with the seaп¬Ӯoor and lava п¬Ӯow layers; a lower wavenumber compared to the overlying features, indicative of a broader-scale transition (Supplementary Fig. 3). These characteristics facilitate the identiп¬Ғcation of the LAB in the seismic sections, ensuring its proper distinction from overlying and underlying events. Injected melt sill identiп¬Ғcation. Melt sills injected into the upper crust exhibit seismic properties similar to the melt sills in the magma domain but can be differentiated through their relative position to the identiп¬Ғed LAB; comparable amplitude and phase characteristics, with a positive-to-negative phase transition (Supplementary Fig. 3); their spatial locations nearly parallel to lava п¬Ӯow layering (e.g., Fig. 7). By leveraging these distinguishing features, these upward and outward injected melt sills can be systematically identiп¬Ғed and differentiated from the melt sills below the LAB in the magma domain, enhancing the geological interpretation of the seismic dataset. Travel-time tomography and full waveform inversion of 2D data The conventional seismic image shows the presence of an event, which is generally interpreted as Layer 2A event (Fig. 1a), but we do not observe any such event in the 3D PSDM image (Figs. 3вҖ“8). To understand the origin of this event, a high-resolution P-wave velocity was constructed beneath the seaп¬Ӯoor, using a combination of travel time tomography and full waveform inversion (FWI) applied to a 2D longoffset multi-channel seismic (MCS) proп¬Ғle (2003) collected during the same survey (See Supplementary Fig. 1 for the location). For the 2D acquisition, the streamer length was increased to 12 km, and both sources were combined to create one larger source. This proп¬Ғle is nearly coincident with Xline 2448 in the 3D box. Initial velocity model. The initial velocity model for travel time tomography was extracted from the velocity model used in the preArticle https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-026-70033-x Nature Communications| (2026) 17:3512 processing for 3D PSDM (Supplementary Fig. 10) but was resampled along the 2D proп¬Ғle. To ensure compatibility with the travel time inversion, the velocity model was extrapolated and interpolated (Supplementary Fig. 20a). Travel time tomography. To construct a robust initial model for FWI, we п¬Ғrst employed an iterative linearised travel time tomography approach63 (Supplementary Fig. 20b). Travel times of п¬Ғrst arrival were manually picked for every fourth shot gather, ensuring that at least one shot was located within the п¬Ғrst Fresnel zone at the seaп¬Ӯoor. Following the approach of

title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
text: initial model for FWI, we п¬Ғrst employed an iterative linearised travel time tomography approach63 (Supplementary Fig. 20b). Travel times of п¬Ғrst arrival were manually picked for every fourth shot gather, ensuring that at least one shot was located within the п¬Ғrst Fresnel zone at the seaп¬Ӯoor. Following the approach of Arnulf et al.32, a uniform travel time uncertainty of 12 ms was assigned to all picked arrivals. In regions affected by shadow zones, п¬Ғrst-arrival refractions were traced to the last reliably observed time, and reп¬Ӯections from the melt sill-LAB were picked at far offsets (e.g., Supplementary Figs. 21, 22). After seven iterations, the ПҮВІ misп¬Ғt was reduced to ~10% of its initial value (Supplementary Fig. 23a). The п¬Ғnal inverted velocity model is shown in Supplementary Fig. 20b. Full waveform inversion After determining the п¬Ғnal tomographic velocity model, FWI64 was performed on the 2D dataset (Supplementary Figs. 21, 22). A careful time-windowing strategy was employed, preserving all refracted and reп¬Ӯected wave information. A Butterworth low-pass п¬Ғlter with a cut-off frequency of 3вҖ“6 Hz was applied to the seismic data before the inversion (e.g., Supplementary Figs. 21, 22). A source wavelet was extracted from the stacked zerooffset seaп¬Ӯoor reп¬Ӯections. The L2 norm (true amplitude) objective function was used to quantify the discrepancy, and the nonlinear conjugate gradient method was used to iteratively update the model64. A total of 20 iterations were performed, progressively reп¬Ғning the model to enhance data п¬Ғt (Supplementary Fig. 23b). The п¬Ғnal inverted velocity model is shown in Supplementary Fig. 20c and the velocity anomaly in Supplementary Fig. 20d. A set of 1D velocity-depth proп¬Ғles along proп¬Ғle 2003 is shown in Supplementary Fig. 8. Checkerboard test. To assess the resolution of FWI, we performed checkerboard tests using three different checkerboard sizes, 1.5 km Г— 1 km, 1 km Г— 1 km and 0.5 km Г— 0.5 km with Вұ5% of sinusoidal perturbation added to the smoothed version of the п¬Ғnal velocity model. We computed synthetic seismograms using these perturbed models and performed FWI starting from the smoothed velocity model, keeping all the parameters the same as the FWI of the real seismic data. The result of inversion is shown in Supplementary Fig. 24, which shows that velocity anomaly of the size 1 km Г— 1 km could be resolved down the base of the model and of the size 0.5 km Г— 0.5 km could be resolved down 1.5

title: Oceanic upper crustal accretion by melt sill and lava flow interaction at Axial volcano
text: the real seismic data. The result of inversion is shown in Supplementary Fig. 24, which shows that velocity anomaly of the size 1 km Г— 1 km could be resolved down the base of the model and of the size 0.5 km Г— 0.5 km could be resolved down 1.5 km below the seaп¬Ӯoor. As the Layer 2 A/2B boundary is laterally continuous, we can resolve this boundary within a vertical uncertainty of Вұ0.5 km. Porosity estimation To quantify variations in rock versus water content near the conventional Layer 2A/2B boundary, a differential effective medium (DEM) approach was employed65 to establish velocity-porosity relationships under different inverse aspect ratio (Оұ) conditions (Supplementary Fig. 8b). The inverse aspect ratio is the ratio of the polar to the equatorial radius and is a measure of pore or crack shape. In our model, seawater properties were set to: P-wave velocity of 1.5 km/s and S-wave velocity of 0 km/s, with a density of 1.03 Г— 103kg/m3. The surrounding basalt matrix was assigned a P-wave velocity of 6.8 km/s, an S-wave velocity of 3.8 km/s, and a density of 2.9 Г— 103kg/m3. These parameters provided a physically consistent framework for evaluating porosity variations based on seismic velocity measurements. In the case of Оұ = 542, porosity near the conventional Layer 2A/2B boundary in FWI result decreased from 18% from seaп¬Ӯoor to 4% at ~1.5 km depth (Supplementary Fig. 8).
