title: Tectonic Stress as the Driving Mechanism for Dike Opening in an Oblique Rift Setting: A Deformation Model of the 2021 Fagradalsfjall Dike, Iceland
text: Tectonic Stress as the Driving Mechanism for Dike Opening in an Oblique Rift Setting: A Deformation Model of the 2021 Fagradalsfjall Dike, Iceland Sonja H. M. Greiner1,2,3 , Freysteinn Sigmundsson1 , HalldГіr Geirsson1 , Steffi Burchardt2,3 , and Olivier Galland4 1Nordic Volcanological Center, Institute of Earth Sciences, University of Iceland, ReykjavГӯk, Iceland, 2Department of Earth Sciences, Uppsala University, Uppsala, Sweden, 3Center for Natural Hazard and Disaster Science (CNDS), Uppsala, Sweden, 4Department of Geosciences, Physics of Geological Processes, The NJORD Center, University of Oslo, Oslo, Norway Abstract Repeated dikeвҖҗintrusions often occur in zones where extensional stress has accumulated. Still, geodetic modeling of observed dikeвҖҗinduced ground deformation often ignores the contribution of tectonic stress. On the obliquely spreading Reykjanes Peninsula, Iceland, tectonic strain buildвҖҗup had been geodetically documented for three decades when a magmatic dike formed at Fagradalsfjall in 2021. We explore the contribution of tectonic stress on dike emplacement in a viscoelastic threeвҖҗdimensional FiniteвҖҗElement deformation model. Tectonic stress accumulation is initially simulated through plate motion, and later partially released by opening of a segmented rectangular dike. We find that surface deformation can be largely reproduced by releasing вҲј60% of the accumulated tectonic stress. Partial stress release and low magma overpressure are consistent with successive dike intrusions and lowвҖҗintensity eruptions in the area. Our model provides an approach to consistently model stressвҖҗrelease constrained by surface deformation. Plain Language Summary In areas where two tectonic plates move apart, tension builds up in the Earth's crust, which can be relaxed by the repeated formation of magmaвҖҗfilled cracks. Although it is well known that stress influences the way in which magma moves through the Earth's crust, this is rarely taken into account in models. In 2021, a magmaвҖҗfilled crack formed at Fagradalsfjall on the Reykjanes Peninsula, SW Iceland. We simulate the tension that built up in the past 800 years and investigate the opening of this crack and how it affected movement of the ground. Our model has two steps: first, tension builds up because of the plates drifting apart, then a part of this tension is released by the opening of a crack resembling the one in Fagradalsfjall. We find that our model predicts ground motion similar to what was measured in 2021 if the crack releases ca. 60% of the builtвҖҗup tension. Models like this help to understand the complex interplay between plate movements and magma moving through the crust. 1. Introduction Magmatic

title: Tectonic Stress as the Driving Mechanism for Dike Opening in an Oblique Rift Setting: A Deformation Model of the 2021 Fagradalsfjall Dike, Iceland
text: the one in Fagradalsfjall. We find that our model predicts ground motion similar to what was measured in 2021 if the crack releases ca. 60% of the builtвҖҗup tension. Models like this help to understand the complex interplay between plate movements and magma moving through the crust. 1. Introduction Magmatic dikes are a key mode of magma transport through the Earth's crust (Breitkreuz & Rocchi, 2018; Burchardt, 2018; Rivalta et al., 2015). They are strongly influenced by the governing stress field, mechanical properties of the crust, and preвҖҗexisting host rock structures (Anderson, 1951; Bertelsen et al., 2021; P. T. Delaney et al., 1986; Greiner et al., 2023; Krumbholz et al., 2014). Tectonic stress and preвҖҗexisting structures such as faults can affect the orientation and shape of magmatic dikes as well as propagation dynamics, all of which are important when it comes to hazard assessment (Buck et al., 2006; Sigmundsson et al., 2024). Gradual stress/strain accumulation at plate boundaries, punctuated by rapid release, has long been acknowledged (e.g., Reid, 1910). Diking during rifting episodes plays a similar role in the deformation cycle as large earthquakes, that is, as modes of stress release (e.g., J. R. Delaney et al., 1998). The Reykjanes Peninsula, SW Iceland (Figure 1a) connects the divergent plate boundary across Iceland to the submarine Reykjanes Ridge (RR) (e.g., Einarsson, 2008; Sigmundsson, Einarsson, et al., 2020). Oblique plate spreading locally rotates the principal stresses and strain accumulation has been geodetically documented during past decades (Clifton & Schlische, 2003; HreinsdГіttir et al., 2001; Keiding et al., 2008). Eruptions in enвҖҗechelon arranged systems on the peninsula (Figure 1a) occur in phases lasting a few hundred years, followed by nonвҖҗ eruptive periods of 800вҖ“1,000 years (SГҰmundsson et al., 2020). After 800 years since the last eruption on the Key Points: вҖў We model the interaction between diking and tectonic stress in the oblique rift setting of the Reykjanes Peninsula, Iceland вҖў Tectonic stress release can explain the 2021 FagradalsfjallвҖҗdike emplacement and associated surface deformation вҖў Our model of tectonicвҖҗdriven diking predicts shearing along the dike, and similar surface deformation as magma overpressureвҖҗdriven models Supporting Information: Supporting Information may be found in the online version of this article. Since then, repeated eruptions occurred and more than 10 dikes have formed in two volcanic systems (Geirsson et al., 2024; Parks et al., 2023; Sigmundsson et al., 2024). As intruding dikes successively release tectonic stress, the importance of magmatic overpressure may increases, which affects the likelihood of magma reaching the surface (Buck et al., 2006). Ground deformation provides insights into subsurface magmatic processes. Most dikeвҖҗinduced surface deformation models fall into two categories: (a) kinematically driven models with uniform or distributed opening/ shearing (Amelung et al., 2000; MontgomeryвҖҗBrown et al., 2010; Okada, 1985; Sigmundsson et al., 2022) and (b) tractionвҖҗbased models, where the intrusion opens due to preвҖҗstress on the dike plane (Currenti et al., 2011; Fukushima et al., 2005; Heimisson & Segall, 2020; Hooper et al., 2011; Yun et al., 2006). While low computation times make the first group especially valuable during volcanic unrest, they require simplifications regarding the intrusion and surrounding crust (Masterlark, 2007; Pascal et al., 2013). In contrast, tractionвҖҗdriven models using the Boundary Element Method or the FiniteвҖҗElement Method (FEM) can consider uniform or variable material properties and stress along the dike due to for example, magmatic overpressure, magma buoyancy or tectonics (Currenti et al., 2011; Heimisson & Segall, 2020; Hooper et al., 2011). We simulate dike opening driven by tectonic stress

title: Tectonic Stress as the Driving Mechanism for Dike Opening in an Oblique Rift Setting: A Deformation Model of the 2021 Fagradalsfjall Dike, Iceland
text: Boundary Element Method or the FiniteвҖҗElement Method (FEM) can consider uniform or variable material properties and stress along the dike due to for example, magmatic overpressure, magma buoyancy or tectonics (Currenti et al., 2011; Heimisson & Segall, 2020; Hooper et al., 2011). We simulate dike opening driven by tectonic stress in a FEM model, which is applied to the magmatic dike that propagated in early 2021 at Fagradalsfjall on the Reykjanes Peninsula. We explore the effect of tectonic stress on dike opening and surface deformation, and quantify the stress release. Centuries of stress buildup since the last eruption and the geodetically well documented preвҖҗ and coвҖҗdiking surface deformation provide an excellent case study and help to evaluate the contribution of tectonic stress relative to magmatic pressure as driving mechanisms in the onset of a rifting episode. 2. Methods 2.1. Tectonic Model Plate spreading has been well documented on the Reykjanes Peninsula through ground deformation and seismicity for three decades prior to 2021 (HreinsdГіttir et al., 2001; Keiding et al., 2008, 2009; Vadon & Sigmundsson, 1997). This allows to compare modeled preвҖҗdiking surface velocities to Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) observations. Our FiniteвҖҗElement models were implemented using COMSOL Multiphysics version 6.1 (see Text S2 in Supporting Information S1 for modeling approach). Further analysis was done in Matlab. To create a realistic tectonic stress distribution, which considers partial stress relaxation at depth, we implemented a threeвҖҗdimensional variable MaxwellвҖҗviscoelastic rheology. Elastic moduli were calculated from a 1D density model by Sigmundsson, Pinel, et al. (2020) (Figure 1c) and pвҖҗwave velocities by Weir et al. (2001). Weir et al. (2001) estimated a seismic velocity ratio of vp/vs = 1.78, corresponding to a Poisson's ratio of ОҪ = 0.27. The resulting Young's modulus (Figure 1d) is depthвҖҗdependent. The viscosity depends on the temperature and confining pressure, which we set equal to the lithostatic stress. We derived a 3D temperature model from a steadyвҖҗstate heatвҖҗtransfer model, where we applied a depthвҖҗdependent temperature profile (Figure 1e) along the plate boundary axis (constrained by geodetic observations by Michalczewska et al., 2014). Heat was transported through advection and conduction (Text S3 in Supporting Information S1). The derived temperatures were related to the viscosity О· based on diffusion creep (Turcotte & Schubert, 2014): О· = RTh2 24VaD0 exp (Ea + pVa ) (1) We assumed dry conditions for the crust and mantle with the following parameters: activation energy Ea =

text: (Text S3 in Supporting Information S1). The derived temperatures were related to the viscosity О· based on diffusion creep (Turcotte & Schubert, 2014): О· = RTh2 24VaD0 exp (Ea + pVa ) (1) We assumed dry conditions for the crust and mantle with the following parameters: activation energy Ea = 300 kJ/mol, activation volume Va = 6 cm3/mol, grain size h = 3 mm, frequency factor D0 = 10вҲ’4 m2/s (Karato & Wu, 1993; Turcotte & Schubert, 2014; Turner et al., 2017). The lowest values of the viscosity ( вҲј1018Pa s) match estimates derived from glacioisostatic adjustment around Iceland (e.g., Auriac et al., 2013). We define the 1021 Pa s isosurface as the base of the elastically deforming crust (Figure 1f). No vertical motion was allowed along the 1021 Pa sвҖҗisoviscous surface. This approach was found to be the only one of several approaches tested that created sufficient focusing of plate boundary deformation to match geodetic observations of plate movements (Text S6 in Supporting Information S1), while allowing for the later implementation of a dikeвҖҗopening model (see also first paragraph in the Discussion). Plate motion was simulated for 800 years. Although the lithostatic stress affects the viscosity, we did not consider gravity and all stresses are deviatoric. We furthermore neglected other processes, which may affect the

title: Tectonic Stress as the Driving Mechanism for Dike Opening in an Oblique Rift Setting: A Deformation Model of the 2021 Fagradalsfjall Dike, Iceland
text: while allowing for the later implementation of a dikeвҖҗopening model (see also first paragraph in the Discussion). Plate motion was simulated for 800 years. Although the lithostatic stress affects the viscosity, we did not consider gravity and all stresses are deviatoric. We furthermore neglected other processes, which may affect the local stress field for example, seismicity, glacioвҖҗisostatic adjustment or geothermal processes. 2.2. Dike Model Dike opening in the stress/strain field created by the tectonic model after 800 years of spreading was simulated by introducing a crack boundary condition on a vertical dike, with two 4.5 km long segments: A northern segment from 1 to 7 km depth, striking N45В°E, and a southern segment from 1 to 6 km depth, striking N23.5В°E. This is a slightly modified version of the dike plane inferred by Sigmundsson et al. (2022) from geodetic observations (Figure 1b). Their model found mostly minor opening in the top kilometer of the crust. We fixed the upper edge of the dike at 1 km depth to ensure our model predicted observed subsidence above it. When compared to their model, the lower edge of our northern segment is 0.5 km shallower so it is not too close to the 1021 Pa s isosurface to prevent unrealistic results. At t = 800 years, the crack boundary condition on the dike plane was activated, releasing stress and decoupling displacements on either side of the dike. The dike displacement field was extracted after a time step of 0.1 years, so it is essentially elastic as only insignificant viscous relaxation occurs in the model over that time interval. Dike opening was driven by tectonic loading, that is, we did not consider effects of magmatic overpressure (in excess of lithostatic pressure) or buoyancy. The emplacement of additional dikes after 2021 indicates that only a part of the accumulated tectonic stress was released by the first intrusion. We therefore applied a negative pressure condition onto the dike segments to control the released fraction f of the tectonic stress, so that only a part of the tectonic stress was released by the dike. We varied f in 10%вҖҗincrements to find which value fits the observed data best (Text S10.1 in Supporting Information S1). The negative pressure only affected the component of the stress field oriented normal to the dike planes. We did not consider dynamic propagation of the dike and instead simulated instantaneous opening. Surface deformation

title: Tectonic Stress as the Driving Mechanism for Dike Opening in an Oblique Rift Setting: A Deformation Model of the 2021 Fagradalsfjall Dike, Iceland
text: in 10%вҖҗincrements to find which value fits the observed data best (Text S10.1 in Supporting Information S1). The negative pressure only affected the component of the stress field oriented normal to the dike planes. We did not consider dynamic propagation of the dike and instead simulated instantaneous opening. Surface deformation predicted by the dike model was compared to two geodetic data sets by Sigmundsson et al. (2022): GNSS data and a SentinelвҖҗ1 interferogram (track T16), after removing the deformation of the two largest earthquakes (Figure 1b and Text S4 in Supporting Information S1). We also compared the dike model to a model driven by uniform magmatic overpressure, which we chose based on minimizing the difference to the stressвҖҗdriven model. 3. Results 3.1. Tectonic Model The viscosity model is asymmetric around the plate boundary with higher viscosity deeper to the north compared to south (Figure 1f), caused by the temperature model and curved plate boundary. Thermal energy collects on the Figure 1. (a) The Reykjanes Peninsula in SW Iceland. Inlet: Volcanic systems across Iceland and the Reykjanes Ridge. text: See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governed by the applicable Creative Commons License southern, concave side of the plate boundary axis due to conductive heat transport, but is dispersed in the northern, convex side (Figure S2 in Supporting Information S1). Along the peninsula, the least compressive principal stress is largest at the plate boundary axis (Figures 1g and 1h). It is higher in the western portion of the peninsula and offshore along the RR where plate spreading is less oblique (Figure 1g). The modeled least compressive principal stress Пғ3 is directed toward вҲјN130В°E. The part of the model below the 1021 Pa sвҖҗisosurface relaxes stress faster than it is accumulated by plate motion (Figure 1h). 3.2. Dike Model The observed surface deformation was best reproduced (Figure 2) when f вҲј60% of the dikeвҖҗnormal component of the tectonic stress were released. The bestвҖҗfitting value ranges between 40 вҲ’ 60% depending on which observational data our model is compared to (Text S10.1 in Supporting Information S1). Modeled deformation shows outward horizontal motion away from the dike, combined with subsidence above the intrusion flanked by uplift (Figure 2a). This pattern is frequently associated with dikes (e.g., Okada, 1985; Sigmundsson et al., 2015). Projection onto the LineвҖҗofвҖҗsight (LOS) vector of the SentinelвҖҗ1 interferogram shows motion toward the satellite northwest of the dike, while the crust above the dike moves away from it (Figure 2b). Southeast of the dike, motion is partially toward and partially away from the satellite. This matches InSAR observations spanning the unrest period and 2 days into the eruption (Figure 2c). The LOS residual (Figure 2d) shows a subsidence signal above the southern segment. There is also a residual signal symmetric around the plate boundary axis, especially southwest of the dike. This signal locally coincides with observed fault movements during dike emplacement (Figure S13 in Supporting Information S1). Observed horizontal GNSS displacements close to the dike are well reproduced (Figure 2e), but differ in direction and amplitude further west. Vertical deformation is overestimated for nearfield GNSS stations (Figure 2f). The largest dike opening is predicted at intermediate depths on the northern segment (Figure 3a). This is notably not the location of highest normal stress on the dike (Figure S10 in Supporting Information S1). Shearing occurs along both segments, but has opposing directions and higher amplitude on the southern segment (Figure

title: Tectonic Stress as the Driving Mechanism for Dike Opening in an Oblique Rift Setting: A Deformation Model of the 2021 Fagradalsfjall Dike, Iceland
text: largest dike opening is predicted at intermediate depths on the northern segment (Figure 3a). This is notably not the location of highest normal stress on the dike (Figure S10 in Supporting Information S1). Shearing occurs along both segments, but has opposing directions and higher amplitude on the southern segment (Figure 3b). The sharp transition between both segments relates to their differing strikes, but motion is continuous across the connection (Figure 3c). Relative displacement is asymmetric across the dike with more shearing occurring on its western side (Figure 3c). At the segment connection, deformation points toward вҲјN130В°E, which agrees well with the direction of the least compressive principal stress Пғ3 predicted by the tectonic model. Dike opening reduced stress in the vicinity of the intrusion (Figures 3d and 3e). More stress is released at depth compared to the shallow parts of the dike, which reflects the variable stress of the tectonic model (Figure 1h). In the nearfield, the stressвҖҗrelease is limited to the depth range covered by dike opening. Above and below the dike, stress remains unchanged or increased (shown here as negative release). Due to the angled dike geometry, the stress release around the dike is also asymmetric (Figures 3d and 3e). Stress is released across a larger area toward the west and around the northern segment, while it is narrower around the southern one (Figures 3d and 3e). Toward the west, stress is reduced by several MPa until Mt. StГіraвҖҗSkГіgfell, but remains largely unaffected further west. Toward the east, stress along the plate boundary axis is reduced significantly until approximately Mt. HraunsselsвҖҗVatnsfell. 3.3. PressureвҖҗDriven Dike Opening The pattern and amplitude of surface deformation are similar between models driven by tectonic stress and uniform overpressure of 12 MPa (Figures 4aвҖ“4f). The main difference is shear in the NвҖ“S component (Figure 4h, Figure S14 in Supporting Information S1). Uplift west of the dike is more elongated in the pressureвҖҗdriven model compared to the stressвҖҗdriven model (Figures 4c and 4f). The largest opening in the pressureвҖҗdriven model occurs near the top of the dike and is centered around the segment connection (Figure 4j). This differs from the stressвҖҗdriven model (Figure 3a). The pressureвҖҗdriven model shows shear close to the segment connection (Figure 4k). However, although it has the same sense of motion as the stressвҖҗdriven model (Figure 3b), the affected area is much smaller for the pressureвҖҗdriven model, and motion is less

However, although it has the same sense of motion as the stressвҖҗdriven model (Figure 3b), the affected area is much smaller for the pressureвҖҗdriven model, and motion is less than for the stressвҖҗdriven model and equal for both segments. Discussion Modeling strain localization in a physically consistent manner rather than forcing it through boundary conditions is generally challenging (e.g., Mulyukova & Bercovici, 2019) and studies on similar spatial dimensions as the present study have used various approaches (Lanzi et al., 2024; R. Pedersen et al., 2009; Turner et al., 2017; van Wijk & Blackman, 2005). We applied a prescribed velocity profile at the base of the elastic crust to localize the plate boundary strain into a narrow region centered on the plate boundary axis. We find that this artificial boundary condition locally affected dikeвҖҗinduced surface deformation by up to 15 mm (Text S5 in Supporting Information S1), which is small compared to observed deformation. Our tectonic model does not account for longвҖҗterm coseismic stress release (e.g., BjГ¶rnsson et al., 2020; ГҒrnadГіttir et al., 2004), which leads to overestimation of crustal stress by our model. However, this may be partially balanced by our assumption that all tectonic stress had been released during the last rifting episode. Although the 2021 Fagradalsfjall dike formed over three weeks and possibly partially solidified early on while other parts continued to widen, we did not consider thermal effects. Furthermore, we did not consider the density difference between magma and the surrounding crust that may be depthвҖҗdependent and can influence dikeвҖҗ pressure conditions. In our model, the dike released a fraction of the normal component of the tectonic stress, but the entire tangential component. This is consistent with emplacement of three additional dikes at Fagradalsfjall and that geodetic models of these events do not include shear on their planes (Geirsson et al., 2024; Parks et al., 2023). Releasing

title: Tectonic Stress as the Driving Mechanism for Dike Opening in an Oblique Rift Setting: A Deformation Model of the 2021 Fagradalsfjall Dike, Iceland
text: released a fraction of the normal component of the tectonic stress, but the entire tangential component. This is consistent with emplacement of three additional dikes at Fagradalsfjall and that geodetic models of these events do not include shear on their planes (Geirsson et al., 2024; Parks et al., 2023). Releasing the entire tangential component also agrees with the idea that once magma spreads in the dike plane, that plane cannot hold shear stress. In contrast, the fraction of released tectonic stress and amount of dike opening are influenced by magma availability. The repeated emplacement of dikes during rifting episodes supports the idea that some dikes release only a fraction of the accumulated tectonic stress (Buck et al., 2006; Hamling et al., 2009; Parks et al., 2023; Wright et al., 2012), while, if enough magma is available, it may be released in a single event (Ruch et al., 2016). It should be noted that the bestвҖҗfitting fraction of released tectonic stress (вҲј60% for the present study) depends to some degree on the dike dimensions and the absolute stress before diking (Text S10 in Supporting Information S1). The stressвҖҗdriven dike model reproduces firstвҖҗorder features of the observed deformation field. Two remaining signals include leftвҖҗlateral shear along the plate boundary axis and subsidence around the eventual eruptive fissures (Figure 2d). The residual shear is especially pronounced west of the dike in the area between Svartsengi and Fagradalsfjall. It largely coincides with individual faults that moved during the dike intrusion (Ducrocq et al., 2024) (Figure S13 in Supporting Information S1) and was modeled as slip along the plate boundary segment by Sigmundsson et al. (2022). The subsidence was modeled as a contracting MogiвҖҗsource by Sigmundsson et al. (2022), while HjartardГіttir et al. (2023) suggested a combination of opening of the first eruptive fissure and shallow fault motion. Surface deformation near Svartsengi was likely also affected by shallow fault motion. While our model predicts the largest opening on the northern segment, the geodetic inversion by Sigmundsson et al. (2022) finds maximum opening on the southern segment. However, both models predict similar surface deformation. Their model could have locally overestimated opening due to the contracting pointвҖҗsource, which is located directly above their predicted area of maximum opening. That the eruptive fissures opened above the southern segment could relate to the presence of preвҖҗexisting fractures and faults above the southern segment (Einarsson et al., 2023; HjartardГіttir et

title: Tectonic Stress as the Driving Mechanism for Dike Opening in an Oblique Rift Setting: A Deformation Model of the 2021 Fagradalsfjall Dike, Iceland
text: could have locally overestimated opening due to the contracting pointвҖҗsource, which is located directly above their predicted area of maximum opening. That the eruptive fissures opened above the southern segment could relate to the presence of preвҖҗexisting fractures and faults above the southern segment (Einarsson et al., 2023; HjartardГіttir et al., 2023). Sigmundsson et al. (2022) modeled the 2021 dike as a combination of opening and rightвҖҗlateral shear, while leftвҖҗlateral shear was only attributed to the plate boundary, resulting in possible tradeoff in shear between the plate boundary and the dike segments. In contrast, in the present study, the dike segments have opposing shear, the direction and amplitudes of which are consistent with expected motion based on the respective segment strike relative to the principal stress axes (P. T. Delaney et al., 1986; Jolly & Sanderson, 1997). The maximum compressive principal stress Пғ1 вҲјN40В°E strikes close to the average of the strike of both segments (N34В°E), but closer to the northern one, which strikes N45В°E compared to N23.5В°E for the southern segment (Figure S10b in Supporting Information S1). Overall, observations of the 2021 Fagradalsfjall dike were largely reproduced by our model driven by tectonic stress without requiring magmatic overpressure. A strong contribution of tectonic stress relative to magmatic overpressure is supported for the 2021 eruption by low initial eruptive flow rates (G. B. M. Pedersen et al., 2022). A purely pressureвҖҗdriven dike can produce a similar deformation field apart from a shearing signal directly above the dike. Such signals may however be difficult to resolve if the dike is accompanied by intense seismicity and nearвҖҗsurface fault motion. Despite similar surface deformation patterns, the modeled opening of the dike plane varies significantly; a reminder of the nonвҖҗuniqueness of deformation models. Stress change in a diking event may influence the location of future events. Magmatic activity jumped westwards to Svartsengi in fall 2023 (Sigmundsson et al., 2024), where a major dike injection occurred in a region of slightly increased tensile stress due to the 2021 Fagradalsfjall dike (Figure 3e, Figure S12 in Supporting Information S1), as also noted by BirgisdГіttir (2023). In models like the one presented here, stress changes can be directly considered in successive deformation models. As the role of tectonic stress relative to magmatic overpressure changes, the risk of magma reaching the surface in highвҖҗdischarge eruptions increases (e.g., Buck et al., 2006; Wright et al., 2012). 5. Conclusions We explored the importance of tectonic stress as a driver for the 2021 Fagradalsfjall dike, using FEM deformation models. Reasonable fits to observations were produced without applying magmatic overpressure, highlighting the importance of tectonic stress for dike propagation. The model suggests that about 60% of the accumulated tectonic stress were released by the dike. Partial release of tectonic stress is supported by the emplacement of three additional dikes between 2021 and 2023. PressureвҖҗdriven models can produce similar surface deformation as stressвҖҗdriven models, differing mainly in shearing signals above the dike, even though deformation in the dike plane can vary significantly. For the stressвҖҗdriven model, the amount and sense of shear relative to opening in the dike plane are consistent with the local tectonic setting. Our study emphasizes the potential of processвҖҗdriven deformation models, that allow to directly consider knowledge of the local geology during dike emplacement. (2022) and can be accessed in the supporting information of their study. The pвҖҗwave velocity model used in the present study was published by Weir et al. (2001), the 1DвҖҗ densityвҖҗmodel was published by Sigmundsson, Pinel, et al. (2020) and the values of the temperature at the plateвҖҗboundary axis are found in Islam et al. (2016). Plate spreading velocities from GNSSвҖҗmeasurements are listed in Keiding et al. (2008). The model files created for this study are accessible from Greiner et al. (2025). title: Probing depth and lateral variations of upper-mantle seismic anisotropy from full-waveform inversion of teleseismic body-waves
text: HAL Id: hal-02988737 https://hal.science/hal-02988737v2 Submitted on 9 Nov 2020 HAL is a multi-disciplinary open access archive for the deposit and dissemination of scientific research documents, whether they are published or not. The documents may come from teaching and research institutions in France or abroad, or from public or private research centers. HAL Authorization Probing depth and lateral variations of upper-mantle seismic anisotropy from full-waveform inversion of teleseismic body-waves Stephen Beller, SГ©bastien Chevrot To cite this version: Stephen Beller, SГ©bastien Chevrot. Probing depth and lateral variations of upper-mantle seismic anisotropy from full-waveform inversion of teleseismic body-waves. Geophysical Journal International, 2020, 222 (1), pp.352-387. вҹЁ10.1093/gji/ggaa069вҹ©. вҹЁhal-02988737v2вҹ© Geophys. J. Int. (2020) 222, 352вҖ“387 doi: 10.1093/gji/ggaa069 Advance Access publication 2020 April 20 GJI Seismology Probing depth and lateral variations of upper-mantle seismic anisotropy from full-waveform inversion of teleseismic body-waves Stephen Beller * and SВҙebastien Chevrot GET, UMR 5563, Observatoire Midi Pyrenees, UniversitВҙe Paul Sabatier, CNRS, IRD, Toulouse, France. E-mail: sbeller@princeton.edu Accepted 2020 January 23. Received 2020 January 13; in original form 2019 February 18 While seismic anisotropy can potentially provide crucial insights into mantle dynamics, 3-D imaging of seismic anisotropy is still a challenging problem. Here, we present an extension of our regional full-waveform inversion method to image seismic anisotropy in the lithosphere and asthenosphere from teleseismic P and S waveforms. The models are parametrized in terms of density and the 21 elastic coefп¬Ғcients of the fourth-order elasticity tensor. The inversion method makes no a priori assumptions on the symmetry class or on the orientation of the symmetry axes. Instead, the elasticity tensors in the п¬Ғnal models are decomposed with the projection method. This method allows us to determine the orientation of the symmetry axes and to extract the contributions of each symmetry class. From simple synthetic experiments, we demonstrate that our full-waveform inversion method is able to image complex 3-D anisotropic structures. In particular, the method is able to almost perfectly recover the general orientation of the symmetry axis or complex layered anisotropic models, which are both extremely challenging problems. We attribute this success to the joint exploitation of both P and S teleseismic waves, which constrain different parts of the elasticity tensor. Another key ingredient is

title: Probing depth and lateral variations of upper-mantle seismic anisotropy from full-waveform inversion of teleseismic body-waves
text: able to almost perfectly recover the general orientation of the symmetry axis or complex layered anisotropic models, which are both extremely challenging problems. We attribute this success to the joint exploitation of both P and S teleseismic waves, which constrain different parts of the elasticity tensor. Another key ingredient is the preconditioning of the gradient with an approximate inverse Hessian computed with scattering integrals. The inverse Hessian is crucial for mitigating the artefacts resulting from the uneven (mostly vertical) illumination of teleseismic acquisitions. Key words: Tomography; Seismic anisotropy; Inversion; Lithosphere; Upper mantle. 1 I N T RO D U C T I O N When mantle deforms, its most abundant constituent, olivine, develops lattice-preferred orientations. The crystallographic a-axes of olivine minerals tend to align along the shear direction, producing fabrics that have strong effects on the propagation of seismic waves. This important property of deformed olivine aggregates has been recognized for a long time, and since the pioneering study of azimuthal anisotropy in the Paciп¬Ғc by Raitt et al. (1969), a vast amount of literature has accumulated on this topic. Seismic anisotropy can be studied with a variety of seismic phases, but by far the most popular are Rayleigh waves and SKS waves. Indeed, the variations of the phase velocity of Rayleigh waves with the propagation direction at both global (e.g. Debayle et al. 2005; Beghein et al. 2006; Marone & Romanowicz 2007) and regional (e.g. Endrun et al. 2011; Schaeffer et al. 2016) scales constrain large-scale patterns of azimuthal anisotropy. While surface waves may provide valuable constraints on large-scale anisotropic вҲ—Now at: Department of Geosciences, Princeton University, Guyot Hall, Princeton, NJ 08544, USA. structures under oceanic plates, which are related to seaп¬Ӯoor spreading and present-day plate motion (e.g. Wolfe & Silver 1998), imaging seismic anisotropy for example in subduction zones or beneath continental orogens remains challenging with this approach. Splitting of SKS waves has been also extensively used to characterize apparent seismic anisotropy in different tectonic environments (e.g. Silver 1996; Savage 1999; Fouch & Rondenay 2006; Long & Silver 2008; Long & Becker 2010; Long 2013; Long & Wirth 2013). However, owing to the quasi vertical incidence of SKS waves, these studies suffer from a poor vertical resolution. To overcome this problem, tomographic methods relying on п¬Ғnite-frequency effects on a new seismic observable, the so-called вҖҳsplitting intensityвҖҷ (Chevrot 2000), were developed (Chevrot 2006) and tested on a large data

text: 2013). However, owing to the quasi vertical incidence of SKS waves, these studies suffer from a poor vertical resolution. To overcome this problem, tomographic methods relying on п¬Ғnite-frequency effects on a new seismic observable, the so-called вҖҳsplitting intensityвҖҷ (Chevrot 2000), were developed (Chevrot 2006) and tested on a large data set of SKS waves recorded by the Southern California network (Monteiller & Chevrot 2011). Another study in the same region (Lin et al. 2014) relied on sensitivity kernels computed by normal mode summation (Zhao & Chevrot 2011) instead of the semi-analytical approach introduced by Favier et al. (2004). These two pionneering tomographic studies were able to obtain 3D models of seismic anisotropy beneath southern California, but the depth distribution of seismic anisotropy in these models remained poorly constrained. An important limitation of these early CвғқThe Author(s) 2020. Published by Oxford University Press on behalf of The Royal Astronomical Society. Downloaded from https://academic.oup.com/gji/article-abstract/222/1/352/5822770 by CNRS user on 25 May 2020 Anisotropic teleseismic FWI tomographic studies is that they relied on splitting intensity, which quantiп¬Ғes the difference in phase delays between the two quasi shear waves that are orthogonally polarized. In other words, even if they properly accounted for п¬Ғnite-frequency effects, they still suffered from the well-known limitations of traveltime (or phase) tomography. In addition, since they only considered shear wave splitting information, they only provided (incomplete) information on shear wave anisotropy since only a small fraction of the complete fourthorder elasticity tensor could be retrieved. For example, splitting intensity tomography only constrains the magnitude of shear wave anisotropy and the projection of the fast direction on the horizontal plane. It has very little sensitivity to its dip (Chevrot & Van Der Hilst 2003), and is also not sensitive to compressional wave anisotropy (Chevrot 2006). In principle, full waveform inversion (FWI, Lailly 1983; Tarantola 1984) has the potential to dramatically improve the resolution of tomographic images at all scales (Tape et al. 2009; Fichtner et al. 2010; Zhu et al. 2012; Operto et al. 2015; BozdaЛҳg et al. 2016; Clouzet et al. 2018), with a theoretical resolution limit of the order of the shortest propagating wavelength (e.g. Virieux & Operto 2009). This improved resolution potential stems from the exploitation of both the amplitude and phase of seismic waves, but also, in the case of teleseismic imaging, from the contribution of later arrivals that are reп¬Ӯected and/or converted on the main lithospheric discontinuities,

title: Probing depth and lateral variations of upper-mantle seismic anisotropy from full-waveform inversion of teleseismic body-waves
text: the shortest propagating wavelength (e.g. Virieux & Operto 2009). This improved resolution potential stems from the exploitation of both the amplitude and phase of seismic waves, but also, in the case of teleseismic imaging, from the contribution of later arrivals that are reп¬Ӯected and/or converted on the main lithospheric discontinuities, such as the Moho (Bostock et al. 2001; Pageot et al. 2013; Monteiller et al. 2015; Beller et al. 2018a). In the last few years, modelling of teleseismic wave propagation in 3-D regional models has become tractable, thanks to the development of new hybrid numerical methods (Roecker et al. 2010; Monteiller et al. 2013; Masson et al. 2013; Tong et al. 2014a; Masson & Romanowicz 2017; Beller et al. 2018b). The principle of these hybrid methods is to compute the incident teleseismic waveп¬Ғeld only once and to inject this waveп¬Ғeld inside a regional spectral-element grid (Komatitsch & Tromp 1999) in later simulations. The incident waveп¬Ғeld could be computed in a 3-D global earth model, for example with SPECFEM3D GLOBE (Komatitsch & Tromp 2002) but for computational efп¬Ғciency, 1-D earth models are usually considered. Available techniques to compute the incident waveп¬Ғeld are the direct solution method (DSM, Kawai et al. 2006; Monteiller et al. 2013; Wu et al. 2018), AxiSEM (Nissen-Meyer et al. 2014; Beller et al. 2018b) or the frequencyвҖ“wavenumber method (Tong et al. 2014b, a). In that case, the inп¬Ӯuence of 3-D heterogeneities located outside the regional grid becomes an issue, but it has been shown that they have actually a limited impact on the resulting tomographic images (Masson & Romanowicz 2017). The reason for this is simply that because the regional domain is much smaller than the source-to-receiver distance, each receiver records waves that propagated through the same path outside the grid. Hence, these heterogeneities as well as attenuation affect similarly each seismic record. These common effects in seismic waveforms are thus absorbed by the source wavelet estimation producing a broaden apparent source wavelet. In lithospheric imaging studies, one can thus assume that these effects can be safely neglected. The п¬Ғrst applications of FWI on teleseismic P wave records in the Pyrenees (Wang et al. 2016) and in the Alps (Beller et al. 2018b) demonstrated that these effects have indeed a limited imprint on teleseismic waveforms and that FWI outperforms classical passive imaging approaches. The motivation of this study is to extend this FWI approach, which was so

title: Probing depth and lateral variations of upper-mantle seismic anisotropy from full-waveform inversion of teleseismic body-waves
text: records in the Pyrenees (Wang et al. 2016) and in the Alps (Beller et al. 2018b) demonstrated that these effects have indeed a limited imprint on teleseismic waveforms and that FWI outperforms classical passive imaging approaches. The motivation of this study is to extend this FWI approach, which was so far limited to isotropic media, to the general anisotropic case. Our main purpose is to demonstrate that the same improvement can be expected for imaging seismic anisotropy than the one already achieved in isotropic tomography at the regional scale. The paper is organized as follows. We п¬Ғrst present the theoretical aspects of FWI for anisotropic media, with a particular emphasis on the problem of parametrizing seismic anisotropy. We present the new ingredients that were incorporated into FWI. First, we now consider S and SKS waveforms into the inversion, in addition to P waveforms. The idea is to extend the sensitivity to a larger number of elasticity coefп¬Ғcients, exploiting in particular shear wave splitting and mode conversions (P-to-S and S-to-P). Our choice to keep the 21 elasticity coefп¬Ғcients allows us to make no a priori assumption regarding the symmetry class of the medium or the orientation of its symmetry axes. These informations are retrieved from the decomposition of the elasticity tensors obtained at each node of the tomographic grid, following the approach of Browaeys & Chevrot (2004). Second, we compute an approximate inverse Hessian with the Born approximation to initialize the l-BFGS iterative algorithm. This is a crucial ingredient to equalize the sensitivity to the different elastic parameters. Otherwise, the inversion mainly updates the parameters that describe the vertical propagation of P and S waves. In other words, the approximate inverse Hessian allows us to deconvolve the results of the inversion from the uneven and anisotropic ray coverage of teleseismic acquisitions. We designed different synthetic experiments to demonstrate that FWI allows us to retrieve both the local orientation of the main symmetry axes and the elasticity coefп¬Ғcients expressed in this local natural reference frame, from teleseismic three-component records of P, Sv and SKS waves. An important conclusion of these synthetic tests is the demonstration that FWI can constrain both the azimuth and dip of the symmetry axis and the stratiп¬Ғcation of seismic anisotropy. 2 A N I S O T RO P I C F W I 2.1 Full-waveform inversion Let us deп¬Ғne the waveform least-square misп¬Ғt function C(m) as the

(13) 2.3 Diagonal Hessian pre-conditioning We solve the normal eq. (4) with an l-BFGS quasi-Newton algorithm which implicitly accounts for the action of the inverse Hessian onto the gradient to determine the optimal search direction. The role of the Hessian is crucial in FWI (Pratt et al. 1998; Virieux & Operto 2009; Pan et al. 2016). From a pure optimization point of view, the Hessian improves the convergence rate of the algorithm by accounting for the local curvature of the misп¬Ғt function. Physically, the linear part of the Hessian can be seen as a deconvolution operator which corrects the gradient from limited bandwidth effects related to the sourceвҖ“receiver acquisition (limited wavenumber illumination and geometrical spreading), rescales it to account for parameters dimensionality

title: Probing depth and lateral variations of upper-mantle seismic anisotropy from full-waveform inversion of teleseismic body-waves
text: by accounting for the local curvature of the misп¬Ғt function. Physically, the linear part of the Hessian can be seen as a deconvolution operator which corrects the gradient from limited bandwidth effects related to the sourceвҖ“receiver acquisition (limited wavenumber illumination and geometrical spreading), rescales it to account for parameters dimensionality and reduces interparameter cross-talks. The nonlinear part of the Hessian mostly accounts for double scattering effects (Pratt et al. 1998). The l-BFGS algorithm approximates the inverse of the true Hessian by п¬Ғnite-differences. However, its п¬Ғrst iteration relies on a (sometimes pre-conditioned) steepest descent step, hence possibly driving the solution towards a local attraction basin. The algorithm thus requires a good initial approximation of the inverse Hessian o (Nocedal & Wright 2006, p.140). Provided that the Hessian possesses a dominant band-diagonal structure, a good choice for o is the inverse of the diagonal terms of the GaussвҖ“Newton Hessian (Jin et al. 1992; Alkhalifah & Plessix 2014). In that case, the diagonal terms of HвҲ’1 o correspond to the autocorrelation of the FrВҙechet derivatives, which can be evaluated via a scattering integral method (e.g. Chen et al. 2007): J = Оҙus i (xr, t) = вҲ’   +вҲһ вҲ’вҲһ (KПҒ + Kc) dП„dt, (14) with KПҒ = ОҙПҒ (x) Gik (xr, t; x, t вҲ’П„) вҲӮ2 П„ us k (x, П„) (15) and Kc = вҲ’вҲӮkGin (xr, t; x, t вҲ’П„) Оҙcknml (x) вҲӮmus l (x, П„) , (16) where i corresponds to the receiver component index, Gik (xr, t; x, t вҲ’П„) the receiver GreenвҖҷs functions and us k (x, П„) the incident source waveп¬Ғeld. Computing J from (14) requires a number of forward modelling simulations given by the number of receiver components times the number of sources. The diagonal preconditioner HвҲ’1 o is then evaluated from: o = diag(JtJ + О¶)вҲ’1, (17) where О¶ is a small regularization term introduced to stabilize the inversion. (25) This simple example demonstrates that the number of non-zero elasticity coefп¬Ғcients depends both on the symmetry class and on the orientation of the elasticity tensor. It also demonstrates that it is necessary to keep the 21 elasticity coefп¬Ғcients to describe even a simple hexagonal tensor with a general orientation of its symmetry axis. This implies that we need to invert for the 21 elasticity coefп¬Ғcients even if we only want to recover the hexagonal part of a 3-D anisotropic medium. Note that different complete sets of elastic parameters could be considered. For example, Rusmanugroho et al. (2015) chose to use the parameters deп¬Ғned in Chen & Tromp (2007), which are commonly used in global adjoint tomography (Sieminski et al. 2007a, b, 2009). However, Rusmanugroho et al. (2017) have shown that choosing either the Chen & Tromp (2007) or the Voigt parameters (the Cij coefп¬Ғcients) leads to similar results. From now on, and for simplicity, we will thus consider a parametrization of seismic anisotropy involving the 21 Voigt parameters. Many other simpler parametrizations of seismic anisotropy can be found in the literature. They all share the motivation to reduce the number of free parameters involved in the description of seismic anisotropy, and hence the dimensionality of the inverse problem. However, this comes at the cost of making strong a priori assumptions on the nature of seismic anisotropy. It is also possible to enforce the symmetry class (e.g. hexagonal or orthorhombic) and tensor orientation and only invert for the intrinsicвҖ”naturalвҖ”parameters, a common approach in exploration seismology (e.g. Gholami et al. 2013; Alkhalifah & Plessix 2014). Note that directly inverting for the angles (azimuth and dip) that describe the orientation of symmetry axes invariably leads to strongly non-linear inverse problems. These problems are untractable with gradient or even quasi-Newton approaches, unless some strong simplifying assumptions are made (e.g. assuming that the symmetry axis lies in the horizontal plane). An alternative would be to systematically explore the model space with a stochastic approach (e.g. Monte Carlo algorithm) as in Mondal & Long (2019). However, such methods can only handle inverse problems with a rather small number of free parameters. To summarize, while apparently simpler alternatives do exist for parametrizing seismic anisotropy, they are not adapted for imaging 3-D general anisotropic media. 3.2 Stiffness tensor decomposition Retrieving the symmetry properties of a general fourth-order elasticity tensor is not straightforward. Several authors later generalized this result for lower symmetry classes (Helbig 1995; Browaeys & Chevrot 2004; Moakher & Norris 2006; Norris 2006). The projection method (Browaeys & Chevrot 2004) is equivalent to performing (Voigt) angular average of phase velocities as demonstrated by Norris (2006). Therefore, it seems to be a natural choice in seismic tomography. The decomposition method (Helbig 1995; Browaeys & Chevrot 2004) starts by recasting the fourth-order elasticity tensor as a vector in a 21-D space. The 21 basis vectors correspond to 21 elementary fourth-order elasticity tensors. In this space, any elasticity tensor can be decomposed into a sum of orthogonal tensors belonging to classes of higher and higher degrees of symmetry (triclinic, monoclinic, orthorhombic, tetragonal, hexagonal and isotropic) Downloaded from https://academic.oup.com/gji/article-abstract/222/1/352/5822770 by CNRS user on 25 May 2020 Anisotropic teleseismic FWI Figure 12. (28) Each of these orthogonal tensors belongs to a subspace that is independent (orthogonal) to the others. A higher symmetry approximation is therefore obtained by projecting the original tensor onto the corresponding subspace. Orthogonal projectors

title: Probing depth and lateral variations of upper-mantle seismic anisotropy from full-waveform inversion of teleseismic body-waves
text: such that C = Ctri + Cmon + Cort + Ctet + Chex + Ciso. (28) Each of these orthogonal tensors belongs to a subspace that is independent (orthogonal) to the others. A higher symmetry approximation is therefore obtained by projecting the original tensor onto the corresponding subspace. Orthogonal projectors and expressions of projection matrices to go from one symmetry class to another are given in Browaeys & Chevrot (2004, Appendix A). Once the different symmetry parts have been obtained from these orthogonal projections, it is possible to compute their respective norms and thus the relative contribution of the different symmetry classes to the full tensor. However, in order to be able to apply this tensor decomposition, one п¬Ғrst needs to express the elasticity tensor in its natural or intrinsic reference frame. In other words, we need to apply a solid rotation to the fourth-order elasticity tensor in order to align the coordinate axes with its symmetry axes. The orientation of the symmetry axes can be determined from the eigenvectors of the dilatational dij = cijkk and Voigt vik = cijkj tensors which are secondorder contractions of the fourth-order elasticity tensor (Cowin & Mehrabadi 1987). For a general anisotropic tensor, the three vectors of the Cartesian coordinate system are chosen as the three bisectors of the closest eigenvectors of the dilatational and Voigt tensors (Browaeys & Chevrot 2004). An example of decomposition for a triclinic elasticity tensor is given in Appendix B. 4 S Y N T H E T I C E X P E R I M E N T S We will now present several synthetic experiments to illustrate the potentials and pitfalls of FWI to image seismic anisotropy in the lithosphere and/or asthenosphere from records of teleseismic P and S waves. 4.1 FWI experimental setup Forward modelling is performed using a grid-injection technique (Chevrot et al. 2004; Monteiller et al. 2013; Beller et al. 2018b) to mimic the propagation of an incident teleseismic waveп¬Ғeld within a regional domain. The waveп¬Ғeld in the regional domain is computed with a spectral-element method (Komatitsch & Tromp 1999) in a mesh containing 32 000 P5 hexahedral elements of 10 km size, leading to a total of 4Г—106 degrees of freedom. The overall size of the mesh is 400Г—400Г—200 km along the x-, y- and z-axes, respectively. The duration of one simulation is 150 s for a time step of 0.05 s. In our experiments the data set is composed of P waves with incidence angles of 15в—Ұand 25в—Ұ, Sv wave with incidence angles of 20в—Ұ, and SKS waves with incidence angle of 7в—Ұ, coming from 12 azimuths taken evenly from 0 to 330в—Ұazimuth. These waves are recorded by 225 three-component sensors placed on a regular 2-D grid with a spacing of 25 km, which covers a square area of 175Г—175 km. Each experiment is performed starting from the same homogeneous isotropic model deп¬Ғned by a density of 3 g cmвҖ“3, a P-wave velocity of 7 km sвҖ“1 and an S-wave velocity of 4 km sвҖ“1. We tested inversions with and without Hessian pre-conditioning. For each target model we perform three different types of FWI. First, we apply a regular isotropic FWI, updating for density, P- and S-wave velocities using an l-BFGS optimization algorithm. Second, we apply a FWI updating for density and the 21 elastic coefп¬Ғcients expressed in the local Cartesian coordinate system. Finally, we apply another anisotropic FWI this time pre-conditioned with the inverse of the diagonal elements of the approximate Hessian. For anisotropic FWI, we will mainly focus on the hexagonal part of the elasticity tensors, which is extracted with the projection method. At each iteration, we consider the gradients and models from the four previous iterations in the l-BFGS algorithm for the estimation of the inverse Hessian. To keep the interpretations simple and the comparisons relevant, note that we do not

Convergence criteria are chosen so that inversions stop when the misп¬Ғt attains one per mil of the initial cost function, when the line-search algorithm fails to п¬Ғnd a new iterate, or simply when the computation wall-time is reached, that is after roughly 70 iterations for isotropic FWI and 60 iterations for anisotropic FWI. 4.2 Isotropic inclusion model Because our formulation of ainversions are also compared to pure isotropic FWI tonisotropic FWI involves the complete set of the 21 elasticity coefп¬Ғcients, the dimensionality of the model space is largely increased compared to the classical isotropic case. This naturally raises concerns on our ability to constrain such a large number of parameters from teleseismic records and on potential trade-offs between the different parameters. To investigate the global behavior of anisotropic FWI, we carry out a п¬Ғrst simple synthetic experiment which consists in recovering a purely isotropic target model. This model is designed as a 100-km-wide isotropic cubic-shaped inclusion embedded in an isotropic homogeneous half-space. We impose a density of 3 g cmвҖ“3, and P- and S-wave velocities of 7 and 4 km sвҖ“1, respectively in the background model and perturbations of +10 per cent on these parameters within the cubic inclusion (Figs 1aвҖ“c). The target anisotropic model we aim to recover is composed of three distinct anisotropic blocks containing homogeneous HTI perturbations embedded in an homogeneous isotropic half-space (Fig. 12). To mimic a layered anisotropic structure, we п¬Ғrst introduce a 50 km high and 150 km wide block located at 125 km depth representing the asthenospheric anisotropy. Inside this layer, the intrinsic anisotropic parameters Пө, Оҙ and Оі are вҲ’0.1, вҲ’0.15 and вҲ’0.05, respectively, and the fast axes are oriented along the northвҖ“south direction (Оё = 0в—Ұand ПҶ = 0в—Ұ). On top of this layer, we put two distinct lithospheric anisotropic blocks. These blocks are 50 km high and 75 km wide and have their center located at 75 km depth. In the western block Пө = вҲ’0.08, Оҙ = вҲ’0.1 and Оі = вҲ’0.03 and the fast axes are oriented at 120в—Ұfrom North. In the eastern block, the anisotropy is the same as in the asthenospheric layer but the fast symmetry axes are orthogonal (Оё = 0в—Ұand ПҶ = 90в—Ұ). This challenging test is designed to investigate the ability of FWI to constrain seismic anisotropy in a case where apparent SKS splitting is very weak (Fig. 13a). In contrast, anisotropic FWI shows a slower convergence rate but reaches a much smaller misп¬Ғt value of 8.1 after 59 iterations. Data residuals (Fig. 14b) present azimuthal variations similar to those obtained for the HTI inclusion, with a 2ПҖ-periodicity that is coherent with predicted SKS splitting in the western block. Anisotropic FWI models obtained with the diagonal inverse Hessian pre-conditioner are shown in Fig. 15. For comparison we also show in Fig. 16 the FWI anisotropic model without preconditioning. When using the pre-conditioner (Fig. 15), anisotropic FWI perfectly retrieves the depth and lateral variations of the fast axis directions (panels a and b). Intrinsic anisotropic parameters (panels c, d and e) are also well recovered except for Оі which exhibits signiп¬Ғcant artefacts. The shape of the anomalies remains close to the target model, but the hexagonal reference velocities and density models present strong artefacts (see panels f, g and h). Anisotropic FWI models obtained without pre-conditioning (Fig. 16) are dramatically different. In this case, the inversion completely fails to recover the fast symmetry axis orientation (panels a and b) and the depth and lateral variation of anisotropy. The recovered medium is mainly VTI (panel a) making the azimuthal orientation of the fast axis uninterpretable (panel b). The Оі parameter is not constrained at all and presents strong artefacts. The Пө and Оҙ parameters are also poorly resolved and more importantly have opposite sign to the target model. The reference hexagonal P-wave velocities are highly perturbed and present strong negative anomalies (panel g) while S-wave velocities are weakly perturbed but present visible artefacts. Again, the density is strongly affected. It is clear from these experiments that, despite a fairly good convergence rate, anisotropic FWI without pre-conditioner gives poor results. The diagonal Hessian pre-conditioner is thus a key ingredient of anisotropic FWI. 5 D I S C U S S I O N 5.1 The importance of the pre-conditioner Anisotropic FWI without pre-conditioning failed to recover the three-block target model despite a good reduction of the misп¬Ғt function, which emphasizes the importance of the diagonal Hessian pre-conditioner. Because we invert the whole 21 elastic coefп¬Ғcients, the model space is drastically expanded compared to the isotropic case, which exacerbates the non-uniqueness of the inverse problem. A good reduction

title: Probing depth and lateral variations of upper-mantle seismic anisotropy from full-waveform inversion of teleseismic body-waves
text: three-block target model despite a good reduction of the misп¬Ғt function, which emphasizes the importance of the diagonal Hessian pre-conditioner. Because we invert the whole 21 elastic coefп¬Ғcients, the model space is drastically expanded compared to the isotropic case, which exacerbates the non-uniqueness of the inverse problem. A good reduction of the objective functional can thus be achieved while converging towards a biased model of the subsurface. The model obtained from non-pre-conditioned FWI (Fig. 16) differs from the expected target model but the results of the inversion can be understood with simple physical arguments. Indeed, because the target model is composed of HTI perturbations, characterized by fast horizontal symmetry axes, P waves propagating at near vertical incidence mostly sample slow propagation directions. Since teleseismic P waves are mainly sensitive to the C33 elastic coefп¬Ғcient, anisotropic FWI will principally update C33, keeping C11 and C22 close to their original values in the starting model. Therefore, C33 will be smaller than C11 and C22 in the п¬Ғnal model, leading to an equivalent VTI medium, characterized by a slow vertical symmetry axis. Since the reference hexagonal velocities are deп¬Ғned for propagation along the symmetry axis, in that case the vertical direction, Пө, Оҙ and Оі become positive. This explains the strong imprint of a teleseismic acquisition on the results of FWI, with strong negative perturbations of P-wave velocities (Fig. 16g), and the recovery of a slow vertical symmetry axis (Fig. 16a) with positive Пө and Оҙ parameters (Fig. 16c). The Оі parameter is mainly constrained by the birefringence of S and SKS waves. However, in the three-block model the apparent splitting is weak, and thus seismic anisotropy is mainly expressed on P-wave records (Fig. 14b), which explains the poor reconstruction of Оі . In contrast, when the diagonal Hessian pre-conditioner is used (Fig. 15) the inversion almost perfectly recovers the target model. We recall from Section 2.3 that the Hessian accounts for the limited bandwidth effects of the sourceвҖ“receiver acquisition and reduces Downloaded from https://academic.oup.com/gji/article-abstract/222/1/352/5822770 by CNRS user on 25 May 2020 Anisotropic teleseismic FWI artefacts resulting from the partial (mostly vertical) illumination offered by teleseismic waves. This means that for the п¬Ғrst iterations of FWI, application of the Hessian reduces the sensitivity to C33 and increases the sensitivity to C11 and C22. The search direction is no longer constrained in the vicinity of an equivalent VTI medium. The quality of the pre-conditioner

title: Probing depth and lateral variations of upper-mantle seismic anisotropy from full-waveform inversion of teleseismic body-waves
text: offered by teleseismic waves. This means that for the п¬Ғrst iterations of FWI, application of the Hessian reduces the sensitivity to C33 and increases the sensitivity to C11 and C22. The search direction is no longer constrained in the vicinity of an equivalent VTI medium. The quality of the pre-conditioner is critical for anisotropy imaging as it removes this fast-slow axis ambiguity resulting from the teleseismic acquisition geometry. Another beneп¬Ғt of this pre-conditioner is that it reduces the high sensitivity close to receiver locations and helps to better focus perturbations at depth. The action of the inverse Hessian depends on the chosen water-level parameter О¶, which we took as the value of the Hessian at the center of the model. Increasing О¶ will enhance perturbations close to the surface, while decreasing О¶ will enhance perturbations at depth. From our experience, FWI is not critically dependent on the water-level because the inverse Hessian obtained by l-BFGS quickly improves after only a few iterations. Hence, the pre-conditioner is important only during the п¬Ғrst iterations, and especially during the initial pre-conditioned steepest descent step. 5.2 Resolution potential of anisotropic FWI for constraining the layering of seismic anisotropy and the dip of the symmetry axis To some extent, anisotropic FWI can be compared to vectorial tomography (Chevrot 2006; Monteiller & Chevrot 2011) and adjoint tomography (Sieminski et al. 2008) based on splitting intensity measurements (Chevrot 2000). This observable is homogeneous to the п¬Ғnite-frequency traveltime measurements (Marquering et al. 1999) commonly used in adjoint tomography (Tromp et al. 2005). The resolution improvement of anisotropic FWI compared to splitting intensity tomography is thus analogous to the resolution increase between full-waveform inversion and adjoint phase tomography (Monteiller et al. 2015). Apparent SKS splitting patterns in the HTI and TTI models are very similar (Fig. 7). This stems from the poor sensitivity of SKS splitting to the dip of the fast symmetry axis (Chevrot & Van Der Hilst 2003). In other words, from SKS splitting alone it is not possible to discriminate tilted anisotropy from pure horizontal anisotropy. However, a striking feature of anisotropic FWI is its ability to constrain both the azimuth and dip angles of anisotropy (Figs 8, 9, 10 and 15). This remarkable achievement comes from the exploitation of both P and S teleseismic waves in our synthetic tests. To get further insights into the behaviour of anisotropic FWI, let us examine in detail the

title: Probing depth and lateral variations of upper-mantle seismic anisotropy from full-waveform inversion of teleseismic body-waves
text: to constrain both the azimuth and dip angles of anisotropy (Figs 8, 9, 10 and 15). This remarkable achievement comes from the exploitation of both P and S teleseismic waves in our synthetic tests. To get further insights into the behaviour of anisotropic FWI, let us examine in detail the initial waveform residuals. A 2ПҖ periodicity of P wave vertical and radial component residuals is clearly observed in the TTI case (Fig. 6c). SKS wave residuals are characterized by a ПҖ-periodicity on the vertical and radial components, and a quasi ПҖ-periodicity on the transverse component. On the other hand, in the HTI case (Fig. 6b), no periodicity is visible on the P wave residuals while the three components of SKS waves present a 2ПҖ-periodicity. These azimuthal variations suggest that the sensitivity to dip is mainly carried by P waves. We performed a new TTI experiment by considering only P waves (Fig. 19) or SKS-waves (Fig. 20). When only SKS waves are considered, anisotropic FWI fails to retrieve the anisotropic parameters: only Оі is recovered with a relatively poor spatial resolution. A closer look at recovered dip and azimuth angles shows that despite the sensitivity of SKS waves to the azimuth of the symmetry axis, we recover a mean azimuth of 30в—Ұ, and fail to recover the mean dip of the symmetry axis. In contrast, considering only incoming P waves allows us to recover both the dip and azimuth angles of the fast axis. In this particular case, Пө and Оҙ are fairly well recovered while Оі is only partially reconstructed with strong artefacts. This last synthetic experiment therefore gives further support to the key role played by P waves in the inversion, which should be kept in mind for future applications on real data. 5.3 Can we resolve the whole elasticity tensor with teleseismic acquisitions? The choice of keeping the 21 coefп¬Ғcients of the elasticity tensor is appealing as it greatly simpliп¬Ғes the formulation of the inverse problem for arbitrary anisotropy. However, this raises the question of our ability to resolve them all as required in the general triclinic case (KВЁohn et al. 2015), especially with a coarse teleseismic sourcereceiver acquisition (Beller et al. 2018a). To check if our approach would recover a full triclinic tensor, we apply pre-conditioned anisotropic FWI to the triclinic tensor presented in Section 3.3. Results of anisotropic FWI (not shown) indicate that FWI fails to

title: Probing depth and lateral variations of upper-mantle seismic anisotropy from full-waveform inversion of teleseismic body-waves
text: (KВЁohn et al. 2015), especially with a coarse teleseismic sourcereceiver acquisition (Beller et al. 2018a). To check if our approach would recover a full triclinic tensor, we apply pre-conditioned anisotropic FWI to the triclinic tensor presented in Section 3.3. Results of anisotropic FWI (not shown) indicate that FWI fails to retrieve a triclinic medium using only teleseismic waves. Perhaps further reп¬Ғnements of the technique could improve the results of FWI for triclinic media. For example, adding Sh or surface waves into the inversion may provide additional independent information to better constrain seismic anisotropy, but these extensions are left for future studies. Another possible issue is related to the posterior decomposition of the elasticity tensor. A potential pitfall is to mix parameters resolved with a different spatial resolution, hence estimating new parameters that are potentially biased. It seems that in our synthetic tests, this problem, if present, has a rather limited impact on the estimation of the hexagonal part of the elasticity tensors. To limit these effects, simple solutions would be to smooth the gradients, add some regularization terms to the cost function, or smooth the model parameters just before performing the decomposition. Again, this was not necessary for the synthetic tests that we have considered in this study. To investigate the potential detrimental effects of projecting a full anisotropic tensor on its hexagonal subspace, we compare the results of the VTI inclusion experiment (Fig. 8) to the same experiment performed using a reduced VTI parameterization (Fig. 17). The reduced approach only involves reference P- and S-waves velocities, density, and Пө, Оҙ and Оі , since the symmetry axis is kept п¬Ғxed along the vertical direction. The convergence rate of the reduced approach is similar to the one of the decomposition approach (Fig. 18). The two approaches also give similar results: Пө and Оҙ are quite well recovered while Оі presents artefacts, especially with the reduced approach. The P-wave velocity model obtained with the decomposition method presents obvious trade-offs with Пө and Оі , whereas this is not the case with the reduced method. In any case, this test demonstrates the capacity of the decomposition method compared to the reduced method to retrieve anisotropy models with hexagonal symmetry. Since the fabrics of olivine and pyroxene aggregates in the uppermantle have a predominant hexagonal component (Becker et al. 2006), we thus believe that our FWI method should be sufп¬Ғcient to obtain robust

title: Probing depth and lateral variations of upper-mantle seismic anisotropy from full-waveform inversion of teleseismic body-waves
text: capacity of the decomposition method compared to the reduced method to retrieve anisotropy models with hexagonal symmetry. Since the fabrics of olivine and pyroxene aggregates in the uppermantle have a predominant hexagonal component (Becker et al. 2006), we thus believe that our FWI method should be sufп¬Ғcient to obtain robust and п¬Ғnely resolved images of upper-mantle seismic anisotropy, at least in regions that are covered by dense seismic arrays.
